title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found RSA keys in the wild with suspiciously sparse moduli, regularly spaced blocks of zeros interleaved with random data, traced to certificates from Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP. The bug appears across independent cryptographic implementations that failed in similar ways, suggesting cryptanalytic algorithms could be tailored to this failure mode and that other implementations may carry the same flaw.

title: Apple Patches 30+ iOS, macOS, Safari Flaws, Including AI-Discovered WebKit Bugs
blurb: Apple released security updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS Tahoe, and Safari 26.5.2, patching more than three dozen flaws, including four WebKit bugs found with AI tools from OpenAI Codex Security and Anthropic Claude. None were disclosed as actively exploited, and Apple told Reuters it is shipping fixes faster than before over concerns that AI could shrink the window between vulnerability discovery and weaponization.

title: Imec's 2026 roadmap details 0.3nm nodes by 2038, CFET transistors become viable at 0.7nm — company redefines Moore's Law as cell sizes gain importance for density
blurb: Imec's 2026 roadmap projects 0.3nm (3-angstrom) chip nodes by 2038, with CFET transistors becoming viable around 0.7nm and GAA architectures lasting roughly seven more years. The research org, which guides R&D for TSMC, Intel, Nvidia, Samsung, and ASML, expects contact poly pitch to stop scaling at A10 in 2030, shifting the path to denser chips toward cell-size reductions and new technologies like Hyper-NA EUV lithography.

title: CISA: Windows BlueHammer flaw now exploited by ransomware gangs
blurb: CISA added the high-severity Windows "BlueHammer" vulnerability (CVE-2026-33825) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, confirming ransomware gangs are now abusing the Microsoft Defender privilege escalation flaw. Microsoft patched it on April 14, but attackers had already used it as a zero-day to reach the SAM database and escalate to full SYSTEM control after a researcher leaked proof-of-concept code in protest of MSRC's disclosure process.

title: AirDrop and Quick Share vulnerabilities affect protocols on five billion devices as fixes begin
blurb: Researchers at the CISPA Helmholtz Center disclosed six vulnerabilities in Apple's AirDrop and Google and Samsung's Quick Share, the proximity file-sharing protocols running on more than five billion active devices across macOS, iOS, Android, and Windows. A proximity attacker within wireless range and no pairing can crash Apple's sharingd daemon, taking AirDrop, AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, and Continuity Camera down together, with fixes now beginning to roll out.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Researchers at Zafran Security disclosed DifyTap, a set of four vulnerabilities in the open source LLMOps platform Dify that let attackers read other tenants' private chats, files, and documents in multi-tenant deployments. Dify, which powers over 1 million applications, patched all four flaws in version 1.14.2, and the two most severe carry CVSS scores of 9.1 and 9.4.

title: Meituan open sources LongCat-2.0, the 1.6T, near-frontier agentic coding model that's been leading OpenRouter — trained entirely on Chinese chips
blurb: Meituan open sourced LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter MoE coding model under an MIT license, revealing it as "Owl Alpha," the stealth model that has topped OpenRouter's developer charts for two months. Trained entirely on Chinese chips with a 1M-token context window, it undercuts Western frontier models on price at $0.75/$2.95 per million input/output tokens, with cache hits free and a promo rate as low as $0.30/$1.20.

title: What's new in Git 2.55.0?
blurb: Git 2.55.0 adds a `git history fixup` subcommand that amends staged changes into an existing commit in one step, and automatically rebases any stacked branches that contain it. The release also brings the built-in fsmonitor daemon to Linux via inotify, speeding up `git status` in large monorepos without a third-party tool like Watchman.

title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
blurb: Security firm LayerX disclosed BioShocking, an indirect prompt injection attack that disguises credential theft as a puzzle game, tricking six AI browsers including OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, and Anthropic's Claude extension into copying user logins and sending them to an attacker. Because page content and user instructions reach agents as one text stream, a malicious page can issue commands the agent cannot distinguish from legitimate ones.

title: Popping the GPU Bubble
blurb: Moondream's Photon inference engine tackles GPU bubbles, the idle gaps where the GPU waits on CPU housekeeping between tokens during autoregressive decoding. Their fix, pipelined decoding, launches the next token's GPU work while the CPU is still committing the current one, overlapping the two kinds of work so forwards run back-to-back instead of waiting on each baton pass.

title: BNY Adds USDC to Institutional Custody Platform in Expanded Circle Partnership
blurb: BNY, the world's largest custodian with $59.4T under custody, made USDC the first stablecoin supported on its Digital Asset Custody platform, letting institutional clients hold, mint, and redeem Circle's token alongside traditional assets. The move follows July 2025's GENIUS Act, which authorized federally chartered banks to custody payment stablecoins, and BNY plans to add more issuers over time.

title: BlackRock Adds Ethena's Synthetic Dollar to Its $20T Aladdin Risk Management Platform
blurb: BlackRock added Ethena's USDe synthetic dollar as an approved digital asset on Aladdin, its institutional risk platform used to manage more than $20T across asset managers, banks, insurers, and pension funds. The deal makes BlackRock's tokenized Treasury fund BUIDL the primary backing asset for Ethena's whitelabel stablecoins, marking the deepest tie yet between the world's largest asset manager and a DeFi protocol.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions that hid their payloads inside image and font files using steganography, tracing the campaign, dubbed StegoAd, to a single threat actor active since at least 2021. The extensions posed as ad blockers, VPNs, and translators across an install base of up to 2.6 million, staying dormant through evasion checks before stealing credentials and running ad fraud.

title: Zuck saves Meta bucks by reusing memory from old servers with a custom CXL ASIC
blurb: Meta designed a custom CXL ASIC that lets it reuse DRAM pulled from decommissioned servers, pooling the older memory over the CXL interconnect rather than scrapping it. The chip extends the useful life of existing hardware and adds memory capacity to AI and database workloads without buying new DIMMs, part of Meta's broader push to cut datacenter spending as it scales infrastructure.

title: U.S. offers $10 million for hackers targeting WhatsApp, Signal users
blurb: The U.S. State Department is offering up to $10 million for information identifying members of UNC5792 and UNC4221, two Russian-linked groups that phished the Signal and WhatsApp accounts of government, military, and allied personnel. The encryption itself was never broken; attackers impersonated Signal support to trick targets into surrendering backup recovery keys, compromising thousands of accounts belonging to officials, journalists, and Ukraine-aligned NGOs.

title: Scaling the Horizon, Not the Parameters: Reaching Trillion-Parameter Performance with a 35B Agent
blurb: A 35B Mixture-of-Experts agent, Agents-A1, matches 1T-parameter models on long-horizon benchmarks by scaling agent horizon instead of parameters.

title: VLK: Learning Humanoid Loco-Manipulation from Synthetic Interactions in Reconstructed Scenes
blurb: Researchers introduced VLK, a method that trains humanoid robots for perception-based loco-manipulation using synthetic data instead of human demonstrations. The pipeline uses 3D Gaussian Splatting to reconstruct real indoor scenes, generates 48,000 paired navigation and object-handling trajectories with no human labeling, and transfers the learned policy to a physical Unitree G1 for navigation and single-object transport.

title: Anthropic and Gov. Newsom forge deal allowing California government to use Claude at half price
blurb: Anthropic struck a deal with California Gov. Gavin Newsom to give all state agencies and local governments access to Claude at half price, plus training and support, for tasks like drafting documents and analyzing information. The agreement deepens Anthropic's ties to the state even as the federal government labeled the company a "supply-chain risk" after a clash with the Pentagon, which signed with OpenAI instead.

title: China’s hollow-core fiber trial pushes 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration — milestone targets AI-era networking bottlenecks
blurb: Chinese firm YOFC, with China Telecom and Dekoli, completed the first field trial of hollow-core fiber moving 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles (206.5 km) without signal regeneration, hitting 1.2 Tb/s per wavelength using only standard erbium-doped amplifiers. Hollow-core fiber guides light through air rather than glass, cutting latency and distortion, and the result advances a high-capacity networking technology largely developed outside the Western supply chain.

title: US Supreme Court Just Blew Up EU-US Data Transfers
blurb: The US Supreme Court ruled in Trump v. Slaughter that the FTC may no longer be independent, undercutting the legal basis for the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, which relies on the FTC's independent oversight 259 times. Privacy advocate Max Schrems has asked the European Commission to withdraw its 2023 adequacy decision, a move that would again restrict EU companies from freely sending personal data to US cloud providers.

title: Pollen (CEO Negus-Fancey, CTO Wright) tried to remove article, and Google helped
blurb: Pragmatic Engineer author Gergely Orosz reports that Google removed his 2022 investigation of failed events startup Pollen from search results after a copyright claim he says is bogus, since he is the sole copyright holder. The case shows how anyone can file a fraudulent DMCA notice to scrub unflattering coverage from Google's index, leaving the actual author to fight a slow appeals process.

title: NixOS 26.05
blurb: NixOS 26.05 "Yarara" is out, built from 59,703 commits by 2,842 contributors and adding 20,442 new packages to Nixpkgs. The release switches initrd to a systemd-based stage 1 by default, updates GNOME to 50 and GCC to 15, and marks the final release to support x86_64-darwin before Apple's platform deprecation takes effect.

title: Introducing Computer Use on Gemini 3.5 Flash
blurb: Google integrated computer use natively into Gemini 3.5 Flash, letting developers build agents that see, reason, and act across browser, mobile, and desktop environments. The capability was previously a standalone Gemini 2.5 model, and Google is shipping optional enterprise safeguards alongside it, including user confirmation for irreversible actions and automatic task halts when prompt injection is detected.

title: SWE-INTERACT: Reimagining SWE Benchmarks as User-Driven Long-Horizon Coding Sessions
blurb: SWE-Interact is a benchmark that tests coding agents on multi-turn, user-driven tasks, where a simulated developer starts with vague instructions and progressively reveals requirements, feedback, and revisions instead of handing over a complete spec upfront. Strong single-turn performance does not transfer: top models like Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5 solve about 50% of single-turn tasks but only 25% of the interactive versions, struggling with over-agentic coding and forgotten requirements.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, its new flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier option, and Luna, its fastest and most cost-efficient model. The release starts as a limited preview for trusted partners after coordination with the US government, and OpenAI classifies all three as High capability in cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk under its Preparedness Framework.

title: MCP Server Architecture Patterns for LLM-Integrated Applications
blurb: This paper catalogues five recurring MCP server architecture patterns observed across fifteen production and public servers, documenting each with context, problem, solution, and consequences alongside four anti-patterns and cross-cutting concerns for auth, versioning, and observability. A tool-count study found selection accuracy drops below 90% at 10 to 15 tools per context for Claude Haiku 4.5 and 20 to 30 tools for Sonnet 4, with code and corpus released as a replication package.

title: iOS 26.5.2 has fixes for 25+ security issues on iPhone, details here
blurb: Apple released iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, and macOS 26.5.2, patching nearly 30 security issues with no new features. Most of the fixes address WebKit and WebRTC, alongside several Kernel-related vulnerabilities, and were first shipped in the iOS 26.6 betas before rolling out to all users.

title: India’s central bank mandated use of .bank domains to enhance trust – but its registry leaked sensitive info
blurb: India's central bank mandated .bank domains to boost trust, but the registry itself leaked sensitive information.

title: LongCat-2.0, a large-scale MoE model with 1.6T total and 48B Active
blurb: LongCat-2.0 is a large-scale mixture-of-experts model with 1.6T total parameters and 48B active per token. The sparse design routes each token to a small fraction of experts, keeping inference costs closer to a 48B dense model while the larger parameter pool aims to lift capability.

title: Steamroller becomes first prebuilt gaming PC to ship with SteamOS — Ryzen 9600X, Radeon RX 7600, 16GB DDR5 RAM system available for preorder at $1,299
blurb: Meta PCs opened preorders for Steamroller, the first prebuilt gaming PC to ship with Valve's SteamOS, pairing a 6-core Ryzen 9600X, Radeon RX 7600, 16GB of DDR5, and a 1TB NVMe SSD for $1,299, with shipping starting July 3. Unlike Valve's more compact Steam Machine, the Steamroller uses standard upgradeable PC parts but drops living-room features like HDMI CEC and quick resume.

title: NASA's X-59 "frankenjet" tests supersonic flight without the sonic boom
blurb: NASA has begun supersonic test flights of the Lockheed Martin X-59 Quesst, an experimental jet designed to replace the sonic boom with a quieter thump comparable to a car door closing 20 feet away. The agency plans to tour the aircraft across US cities to gather public feedback, data that could inform a reversal of the FAA's 1973 ban on overland supersonic flight.

title: Amazon EKS now supports control plane egress through your VPC
blurb: Amazon EKS now lets you route Kubernetes control plane egress through your own VPC, applying your security groups and firewall rules.

title: Instagram is incorporating users' photos in ads for Meta Glasses
blurb: Meta is testing Instagram ads for its Ray-Ban smart glasses that pull in users' own photos to personalize the creative. The format stitches a person's existing images into glasses promotions, an early example of generative ads built from first-party account data and a likely preview of how Meta plans to use its image libraries to sell hardware.

title: NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project Pushes Weakened TLS Post-Quantum Standard
blurb: The author alleges that the NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project is paying to standardize "ietf-tls-mlkem," a weaker post-quantum TLS key exchange than the hybrid "ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem," and is now packing an IETF TLS working-group vote with first-time participants after losing an earlier round. The post urges readers to join the mailing list and register opposition to draft-ietf-tls-mlkem-08 before the working-group last call ends on 8 July 2026.

title: When Is a Draft Accepted? A Theory of Acceptance in Speculative Decoding
blurb: A theory of when speculative-decoding drafts get accepted under greedy, relaxed, and tree-based rules, with exact KL certificates validated on Qwen3.

title: SWE-Together: Evaluating Coding Agents in Interactive User Sessions
blurb: SWE-Together is a multi-turn benchmark that evaluates coding agents across real interactive user sessions rather than one-shot task descriptions, built from 109 repository-level tasks curated out of 11,260 recorded sessions. An LLM-based user simulator replays the original users' intents and supplies corrective feedback, scoring agents on both final repository correctness and how many interventions they needed, with stronger agents succeeding more while requiring fewer corrections.

title: A native graphical shell for SSH
blurb: Outer Shell is an open-source graphical shell that lets servers and edge devices serve up a browser-based home screen of apps, each a small dependency-free HTTP server reached over SSH rather than exposed network ports. Apps communicate through Unix domain socket files with filesystem permissions and skip encryption entirely, since the SSH layer handles it, and can register capabilities so one app opens files in another.

title: Import AI 463: Self-improving robots; a 10k Chinese GPU cluster; and an elegiac essay for the human era
blurb: NVIDIA researchers built ENPIRE, a harness that puts physical robots through the same autonomous experiment-and-improve loop coding agents use, with modules for automatic resets, policy refinement, parallel rollouts, and agent-driven code evolution. The system handles trial scoring and scene resets without human judgment, narrowing two long-standing bottlenecks in real-world robot learning, though the authors frame the current results as suggestive rather than proven.

title: Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs
blurb: KDDI disclosed a breach exposing email addresses and passwords for up to 14.2 million customers across six Japanese ISPs; affected users should reset passwords.

title: Highlights from Git 2.55
blurb: Git 2.55 shipped with contributions from over 100 developers, headlined by teaching git repack to write incremental multi-pack index chains directly via `--write-midx=incremental`. The append-only mode adds a new MIDX layer for freshly created packs without rewriting the full index, cutting maintenance costs for large repositories where a single-file MIDX update means a large write.

title: In major privacy win, Supreme Court rules geofence warrants are protected by privacy rights
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that cell-phone location history is protected by the Fourth Amendment, requiring police to obtain a search warrant before forcing companies like Google to hand over geofence location data. The court stopped short of banning the "reverse" warrants outright, instead requiring authorities to narrow their requests, while rejecting the argument that using a service counts as willingly sharing location data.

title: Super Micro Office Raided as Taiwan Expands Chip Smuggling Probe
blurb: Taiwanese authorities raided Super Micro's offices on Monday, expanding a probe into the alleged smuggling of Nvidia chips into China through the company's servers. The widening investigation puts Super Micro under fresh scrutiny over export-control enforcement, as Taiwan tightens oversight of how advanced AI hardware moves toward restricted markets.

title: Qwen Image Agent
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team introduced Qwen-Image-Agent, an agentic framework that wraps a text-to-image model in planning, reasoning, search, and memory to fill in underspecified or knowledge-dependent prompts. The system treats user input as partial context and builds out the full generation context before rendering, reaching state-of-the-art results on the new IA-Bench, Mindbench, and WISE-Verified benchmarks against strong baselines.

title: Supreme Court delivers ‘major win’ for tech privacy in Chatrie ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that police collecting phone location data from a geographic area constitutes a Fourth Amendment search, extending privacy protections to geofence warrants and the data users hand to companies like Google. Justice Kagan's majority opinion signaled that Fourth Amendment limits should keep pace as technology advances, a precedent that will constrain how law enforcement taps tech platforms for location records going forward.

title: Cursor releases iPhone and iPad app following recent acquisition by SpaceX
blurb: Cursor released its first iPhone and iPad app, letting developers launch coding agents, review diffs, merge pull requests, and talk to agents by voice from the App Store. The launch is Cursor's first major release since SpaceX, which includes xAI, acquired the agentic coding firm earlier this month, and it comes with 75% off Composer 2.5 runs through July 5.

title: Prime Day shows how AI is changing shopping, testing Amazon’s bet against ChatGPT and others
blurb: U.S. shoppers spent a record $26.4B across all retail sites during Amazon's four-day Prime Day, and for the first time visitors arriving from AI chatbots were the most likely to buy, converting 40% better than those from search, email, or social, per Adobe. The shift tests Amazon's strategy of locking outside assistants like ChatGPT and Perplexity out of its store while pushing shoppers toward its own Rufus assistant.

title: Crunchbase Data: Q2 Brought The Most Billion-Dollar Startup Exits Since 2021
blurb: Q2 2026 produced the most billion-dollar startup exits since the 2021 market peak, led by SpaceX's record IPO that raised $75B and hit a $2.1T first-day market cap. The bigger trend is size over count, with SpaceX's $60B purchase of Cursor marking the priciest venture-backed acquisition ever and confidential IPO filings from Anthropic and OpenAI now testing the trillion-dollar mark.

title: GROW$^2$: Grounding Which and Where for Robot Tool Use
blurb: GROW², a new robotics method, lets robots improvise tools by grounding open-world affordances, such as using a plate to cut a cake when no knife is available. It splits the task into a vision-language model that picks the object and relevant parts, then vision foundation models that localize precise 3D regions from a single RGB-D image, beating prior baselines in simulated and real-world tests.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of an upcoming powerful AI model, according to a person familiar with the matter. The request comes nearly two weeks after rival Anthropic pulled its most capable offerings from the market under regulatory pressure, signaling that Washington is now shaping the pace at which frontier models reach customers.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub detailed how Copilot stretches each token through prompt caching and on-demand tool search, plus expanding Auto to pick the model per task.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot supply enough compute, forcing Meta to tell staff to use AI tokens more efficiently, the Financial Times reported. Meta is accelerating its shift to Muse Spark, an internal model under Superintelligence Labs, even as Google itself rents 110,000 Nvidia GPUs from SpaceX for $920M a month to meet its own Gemini demand.

title: Rocket Lab buys its way into the satellite big league with $8B Iridium deal
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite operator Iridium in a deal valued at roughly $8B, vaulting the launch company into the operational satellite-services business. The acquisition pairs Rocket Lab's launch vehicles and spacecraft manufacturing with Iridium's 80-plus satellite constellation and recurring connectivity revenue, moving the company beyond launch into owning and running an orbital network.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Spend $520 Billion on Chip Plants in South Korea
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix will spend a combined $520B on new chip plants in South Korea, saying current investment levels can't meet demand amid a global shortage. The world's two largest memory-chip makers are racing to expand capacity as AI workloads strain supply, with the buildout reinforcing South Korea's central role in the memory market.

title: WATaBoy: JIT-Ing Game Boy Instructions to WASM Beats a Native Interpreter
blurb: WATaBoy is a Game Boy emulator that JIT-compiles CPU instructions to WebAssembly bytecode, letting the browser's engine recompile it to native machine code and sidestepping iOS's ban on direct JIT outside web browsers. Built as an undergraduate final-year project, it benchmarks the JIT-to-Wasm approach against a native interpreter and comes out faster, the first console emulator to test the technique against an interpreter running natively.

title: What happens when you run a CUDA kernel?
blurb: This blog post traces a single CUDA vector-add kernel on an RTX 4090 from source code down to the GPU, showing that getting one answer back involves tens of millions of CPU instructions, nine hundred ioctls, and a memory-mapped doorbell register. It walks through the full nvcc compiler pipeline, the PTX and SASS device code it emits, and how the launch reaches the hardware's warps.

title: Kids act would require age checks to get online
blurb: The KIDS Act, which Congress may vote on within a week, bundles a revised KOSA with other internet bills under an expedited process that would pressure online services to verify every user's age. The EFF argues its "knows or should have known" liability standard for identifying minors will push platforms toward blanket age-checking, undermining privacy, encryption, and free expression for users of all ages.

title: iPhone 18 Pro supplier list and parts exposed in Tata data leak
blurb: The ransomware group World Leaks posted files on the dark web detailing iPhone 18 Pro components, supplier names, and photographs stolen from Tata Electronics, Apple's manufacturing partner in India. At least six files map unreleased Pro parts to specific vendors across the logic board, battery, and camera, exposing Apple's sourcing strategy and straining the Indian supply base it is using to diversify away from China.

title: Rocket Lab continues buying spree by acquiring satellite company Iridium
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite operator Iridium for $54 per share, valuing the company at $8B and pushing the launch firm toward becoming a full space-services provider. The deal continues an aggressive 2026 buying spree that already included Motiv, Mynaric, and Geost, and it hands Rocket Lab Iridium's in-orbit constellation plus a valuable bundle of spectrum.

title: Meituan open-sources LongCat-2.0, a 1.6T-parameter model that it says was trained on a 50K-chip cluster of domestic Chinese processors, without giving details (Reuters)
blurb: Meituan released and open-sourced LongCat-2.0, a 1.6T-parameter model the Chinese food delivery company says was trained on a 50,000-chip cluster of domestic processors. Meituan gave no details on the hardware, but the claim signals progress in training frontier-scale models without Nvidia GPUs as US export controls push Chinese firms toward homegrown silicon.

title: Databricks’ Co-Founder Arsalan Tavakoli: Every Software Monopoly Falls in the Next 24 Months
blurb: Databricks co-founder Arsalan Tavakoli told the SaaStr AI stage that any business holding a software monopoly today will lose it within 12 to 24 months, citing collapsing pricing power from cheaper builds, low-end entrants, and easier migrations. He spoke from a $6.9B revenue run-rate growing 80% a year, and argued enterprises are "token maxing" with rising AI spend but no clear read on ROI.

title: The Supreme Court just made it harder for police to track your phone
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that geofence searches, where police ask a tech company for every phone inside a mapped area, require a warrant backed by probable cause under the Fourth Amendment. The decision extends the 2018 Carpenter precedent on cell-tower data and stops short of banning the practice, leaving police free to seek location records if they name a basis and keep requests narrow.

title: Security researchers tricked LLMs into giving them cocaine recipes by abusing role models for prompt injection
blurb: Security researchers bypassed LLM safety guardrails to extract cocaine recipes by exploiting role-play prompts for injection attacks.

title: Deno update streamlines creation of desktop apps
blurb: Deno 2.9 introduces an experimental deno desktop feature that compiles a script or web framework project into a single self-contained native binary, with the UI running in a webview and logic in Deno. The release also cuts hello-world cold start from 34ms to 17ms, holds memory roughly flat under load, speeds up Deno.serve throughput, and advances Node.js compatibility to version 26.

title: Trump administration threatens 92 GW of new electricity supply with red tape
blurb: A new Wood Mackenzie study found that Trump administration permitting changes and federal funding withdrawals threaten 92 GW of planned electricity capacity, after already canceling 7 GW on federal land in 2025. The friction, which puts more than $121 billion in energy investment at risk, lands as AI data centers drive US power demand up for the first time in two decades.

title: Your Space is My Zone: Demystifying the Security Risks of AI-Powered Applications on Pre-Trained Model Hubs
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic security analysis of AI-powered applications hosted on model hubs like Hugging Face, mapping the AI-App lifecycle to OWASP-style taxonomies to identify five threat categories and ten attack vectors. Scanning over 970,000 public apps, the authors found thousands leaking credentials, hundreds vulnerable to code injection, and tens carrying embedded backdoors that suggest active exploitation.

title: Cursor releases its iOS app for vibe coding on the go
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta that turns the phone into a control surface for its coding agents, letting developers launch agents, review diffs, issue voice and slash commands, and merge pull requests from mobile. The app requires iOS 26 and a paid Cursor plan, supports models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Cursor's own Composer, and can run cloud agents in isolated VMs or remote-control sessions on a local machine.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Anthropic's June 2026 Economic Index report finds that Claude usage now mirrors the rhythms of daily life, with work queries dropping on weekends, news requests peaking in the morning, and tax questions surging near filing deadlines. The report also shows that more compute correlates with more valuable outputs, and previews the first results from the Anthropic Economic Index Survey linking self-reported expectations to actual usage data.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across an entire channel. The tool monitors channel activity, sends preset alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drops comments into conversations, and can be directed to fix code issues, pushing Claude further into persistent workplace-agent territory.

title: We’re making Bunny DNS free
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated all DNS query fees, offering free hosting for up to 500 domains with no query limits or per-request billing.

title: What the Saga Over Anthropic’s Mythos Tells Us About the Cyber Risks From AI
blurb: Anthropic has restricted its Mythos AI model to just 200 partner organizations, saying the tool is too effective at finding software vulnerabilities to release publicly. The company warns that in the wrong hands, Mythos could help attackers steal data or disrupt critical infrastructure, framing the limited rollout as a test of how labs handle dual-use security tools.

title: Memory Safe Context Switching
blurb: Fil-C now supports the ucontext APIs (getcontext, setcontext, makecontext, swapcontext) and longjmp/setjmp in a memory-safe way, so misuse that would corrupt the stack in ordinary C instead panics the program. Restoring a dangling stack, freeing a stack still referenced by a context, or swapping into the currently executing context all become safety errors rather than the silent, untraceable crashes that attackers can exploit.

title: South Korean tech giants commit over $550B to ease ‘ RAMageddon’
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix will invest $518B to build four memory fabs in southwestern South Korea, part of a national plan that pushes total industry AI commitments past $900B. The buildout targets RAMageddon, the AI-driven memory shortage now setting record demand for both firms, and adds $356B for AI data centers from SK, GS, and Naver through 2035.

title: Arena, the AI leaderboard everyone uses, is now a $100M business
blurb: Arena, the crowdsourced AI leaderboard born as a 2023 UC Berkeley research project, hit $100M in annualized run-rate revenue just eight months after launching its commercial service. The company sells AI Evaluations, deep-dive model analytics drawn from over 10 million community comparisons, to labs and enterprises, competing for post-training dollars against human-labeling shops like Mercor, Surge, and Scale AI.

title: Cursor now has a mobile app for guiding your coding agent on the go
blurb: Cursor released an iOS app that lets developers prompt and manage coding agents from their phones, spinning up new agents or steering ones started on the desktop client. The launch extends the company's Cursor 2.0 shift toward independent coding agents and follows similar mobile tools from Anthropic and OpenAI, as oversight of code-writing agents increasingly replaces hands-on editing.

title: Base's Two-Hour Outage Exposes Single-Sequencer Risk
blurb: Base went down for nearly two hours after its single sequencer processed one invalid block, halting all blocks until operators manually restarted nodes.

title: DAIN: Dynamic Agent-Based Interaction Network for Efficient and Collaborative Multimodal Reasoning
blurb: DAIN reframes multimodal fusion as dynamic multi-agent collaboration, beating static MoE with sparse activation and a 2.6% ADNI gain.

title: What can you confidently guarantee about your software?
blurb: The author argues that most teams can guarantee far less about their software than they assume, because testing, types, and code review each cover only narrow slices of actual behavior. The piece walks through where common assurance techniques break down and makes the case for being explicit about which properties you can actually prove versus the ones you merely hope hold.

title: Digital Realty falls 5% after taking $3.5 billion stake in Blackstone's Virginia data centers
blurb: Digital Realty bought an $3.5B stake in three Northern Virginia data centers from Blackstone, paying $1.2B in cash and $2.3B in shares for assets valued at $7.8B. The stock fell 5.4% premarket on the dilution, even as the deal deepens its exposure to the world's largest data center market, where 92% of capacity under construction is already pre-leased.

title: Apple just acquired the app that won last year’s ‘Innovation’ Apple Design Award
blurb: Apple acquired Play, the SwiftUI prototyping tool from Rabbit 3 Times that won the 2025 Apple Design Award for Innovation, picking up assets and hiring select staff. Play has been pulled from the App Store, with its real-time Mac and iPhone prototyping features likely to surface inside Xcode or Apple's free developer tools over time.

title: China’s Loongson launches homegrown 16-core server CPU built on LoongArch architecture — 40W chip with DDR4 ECC and 32 PCIe lanes targets cheap SMB file, database, and web servers
blurb: Loongson released the 3C3000, a 16-core server CPU built on its homegrown LoongArch instruction set and aimed at low-cost file, database, and web servers for small and medium businesses. The 40W chip runs at 1.5GHz to 1.8GHz with dual DDR4-2400 ECC channels and 32 PCIe lanes, and is pin-compatible with the earlier 3B6000 so builders can reuse existing platforms.

title: SCOTUS says detailed cellphone location data is protected by the Fourth Amendment
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that police conduct a Fourth Amendment search when they obtain a person's detailed cellphone location history from a tech company, meaning a warrant backed by probable cause is generally required even for short time spans or third-party-held data. The decision does not ban geofence warrants, but it extends constitutional protection beyond the Google case to comparable location records held by Apple and other companies.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
blurb: OpenAI is leaning toward delaying its IPO until next year, with advisers urging Sam Altman to slow down after watching SpaceX's stock turn volatile. The caution comes as the company works through financial challenges, suggesting public markets remain wary of richly valued AI startups despite OpenAI's growth.

title: Linux for the Sega MegaDrive
blurb: Linux runs on a Sega MegaDrive using a Mega EverDrive cartridge for RAM and SD-card file access.

title: Taiwan Steps Up Probe Into AI Hardware Smuggling
blurb: Taiwanese authorities escalated a probe into the unauthorized diversion of advanced AI servers made by Super Micro Computer, part of a wider crackdown on alleged smuggling of Nvidia chips to China. The investigation underscores how export controls have pushed enforcement onto Taiwan's hardware supply chain, where contract manufacturers sit between US chip restrictions and Chinese demand.

title: BT and Verizon spin off international networking arms into $4B joint venture
blurb: BT and Verizon are merging their international networking units into a $4B joint venture. The combined entity pools the two carriers' cross-border enterprise connectivity and managed-network operations, giving multinational customers a single provider as both companies shed assets outside their core domestic markets.

title: Mag 7 value shrinks by $2.3 trillion amid AI spending jitters — but investors are still backing chipmakers
blurb: About $2.3T has been wiped off the Magnificent 7 this month as investors scrutinize the hundreds of billions Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are spending on AI chips and data centers. The CNBC Mag 7 Index is down 10% in June, but semiconductor stocks have held up, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index rising 6% as money rotates toward chipmakers.

title: The Lab Mistake That Might Revolutionize Computing
blurb: Researchers built a neuromorphic device that mimics a biological neuron on a standard silicon chip, an accidental discovery that could sharply cut AI's energy use. The approach reproduces brain-like computing with far fewer transistors than existing CMOS designs, which need so many components per neuron that they cap system size. The brain is roughly a million times more energy efficient than GPUs at comparable tasks.

title: Microsoft builds a bouncer to keep bots out of Teams meetings
blurb: Microsoft is adding controls to Teams that block unauthorized AI notetaker bots from silently joining and transcribing meetings.

title: Inside Rocket Lab’s $8B plan to buy its way past SpaceX
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy satellite operator Iridium Communications for $8B, valuing the company at roughly $54 a share, a 24.1% premium. The deal hands Rocket Lab a network of 2.5M subscribers and licensed L-band spectrum, marking its fifth acquisition in about a year as it builds a vertically integrated rival to SpaceX rather than launching one from scratch.

title: Figma's design agent, now with custom tools and greater context
blurb: Figma expanded its canvas-based design agent with custom tools, skills, and richer context about how a team works. Designers can now prompt the agent to build reusable generative plugins using PropsKit so they feel native to Figma, plus create custom shader effects and fills, without needing a developer or a separate dev setup.

title: Accel backs Pocket’s $11M raise, as the AI hardware startup turns a viral launch into $27M ARR
blurb: Pocket raised $11M led by Accel, with Y Combinator and operator-investors including Vercel's Guillermo Rauch and ElevenLabs' Mati Staniszewski joining the round. The AI hardware startup, which records meetings and generates notes and follow-ups only when switched on, has shipped 35,000 units and hit a $27M annualized revenue run rate, with early enterprise customers like DoorDash.

title: The UK CMA proposes requiring Apple and Google to relax UK developer payment "steering" rules, and Apple to open NFC; Google says it "already made the changes" (Sam Tabahriti/Reuters)
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed forcing Apple and Google to loosen "steering" rules that block developers from directing users to cheaper payment options outside their app stores, and to require Apple to open NFC access for third-party payments. Google said it had already made the changes, while the regulator's proposals stem from new powers to designate both companies with strategic market status.

title: South Korea to spend $1T on more memory chip production and humanoid robots
blurb: South Korea's government and top tech firms committed $1 trillion to flagship projects spanning memory chip fabs, AI data centers, and commercial humanoid robots by 2028. Samsung and SK Hynix account for $585 billion of that to build new plants and double DRAM output within five years, while Hyundai pushes to mass-produce Boston Dynamics robots for factory work.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: Sources: AI video startup Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B pre-money valuation, more than 4x its valuation in a January 2026 round (The Information)
blurb: AI video startup Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B pre-money valuation, more than 4x its January 2026 round. The jump reflects investor appetite for generative video tools, where Higgsfield competes against well-funded rivals like Runway and OpenAI's Sora.

title: Chrome is putting things on your computer you never agreed to
blurb: Two stories this week showed Chrome silently installing software users never approved. Since April, Chrome has quietly downloaded Gemini Nano, Google's roughly 4GB on-device AI model, onto eligible machines with no prompt or notification, while Microsoft researchers separately flagged a malicious extension impersonating Perplexity that logged every query and address-bar keystroke before forwarding traffic to an attacker-controlled server.

title: Comcast Plans to Spin Off NBCUniversal Into New Media Firm
blurb: Comcast plans to spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a new publicly traded media company, separating its content businesses from cable-TV and internet operations. The move aims to lift shareholder value as legacy pay-TV declines, leaving Comcast focused on broadband and the new firm to compete in streaming against Netflix and Disney.

title: One million passports leaked online
blurb: A security researcher found nearly 985,000 passports and photo IDs sitting at unprotected public URLs, exposed by Nefos Solutions software used by cannabis clubs across Spain. The leaked records included names, addresses, phone numbers, and consumption data for visitors worldwide, including 30,000 from the US, leaving the trove free for anyone to scrape and resell.

title: Musk approved to acquire optical transceiver developer taking on Chinese dominance
blurb: The FTC cleared Elon Musk to acquire Mesh Optical Technologies, a startup founded by ex-SpaceX engineers building optical transceivers to challenge China's dominance of the market. Mesh emerged from stealth in February after a Thrive Capital-led Series A, and its Alpha C1 transceiver moves data at 1.6 Tb/s while drawing roughly a third of rivals' power, targeting the high-speed interconnects AI hyperscalers need.

title: Working With AI: A concrete example
blurb: Carson Gross, creator of htmx and hyperscript, walks through a real debugging session where Claude correctly found the root cause of a parser regression but proposed a fix that papered over the problem rather than addressing it. He uses the example to illustrate the "Sorcerer's Apprentice" risk: leaning on AI for solutions you don't fully understand can leave you unable to maintain your own systems.

title: The inside scoop on alerting changes in Kubernetes Monitoring
blurb: Grafana Cloud is consolidating Kubernetes Monitoring alerts onto Grafana-managed alerting, so reinstalls may silently break notifications routed through the old Mimir Alertmanager.

title: SpaceX's $25 billion bond sale drives huge demand - and a potential headache for investors
blurb: SpaceX raised $25B in senior unsecured notes, drawing nearly $90B in orders just two weeks after its record IPO. The offering repays a bridge loan but underscores the company's $5B net loss and doubled capex, while analysts warn that holding both SpaceX equity and bonds concentrates the same execution risk rather than diversifying it.

title: Legacy Nvidia RTX 3060 12GB returns to retail five years after original launch, priced at $339 — resurrected GPU strategy that Jensen called a 'good idea' apparently comes to fruition
blurb: Nvidia's five-year-old RTX 3060 12GB is returning to retail at $339, just $10 above its 2021 launch price, as board partners including Gigabyte and Asus quietly restock the Ampere card. The move follows Jensen Huang's CES 2026 comment that reviving older GPUs on trailing-edge nodes could ease RTX 50-series supply, though the 3060 lands only slightly cheaper than the faster RTX 5060.

title: Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron sued over alleged DRAM price fixing amid record memory costs — lawsuit claims coordinated HBM shift was cover to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 production
blurb: Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron were sued on June 25th in a California class action accusing the three makers, which together control roughly 90% of the DRAM market, of coordinating to restrict supply and inflate prices the complaint says have climbed about 700% over four years. The suit argues their shift toward high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators was cover to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 output, reviving claims a similar 2018 case failed to prove before its 2020 dismissal.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy its Mythos 5 cybersecurity model to a small group of vetted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers, reversing an export block imposed earlier in June over national security concerns. The revised license excludes Fable 5, the weaker variant still awaiting general-release approval, and underscores the absence of a consistent US regulatory framework for frontier AI.

title: Dissecting Apple's Sparse Image Format (ASIF)
blurb: A reverse engineer documents how they wrote a parser for ASIF, the sparse virtual disk format Apple introduced in macOS 26 Tahoe.

title: AI and vibe coding fuel a surge in game releases; ATTN Economy says 181K mobile games launched in six months to May, up 118% on iOS and 73% on Android YoY (Orlando Crowcroft/Financial Times)
blurb: Mobile game releases surged to 181K in the six months to May, up 118% on iOS and 73% on Android year-over-year, with ATTN Economy crediting AI tools and vibe coding for the acceleration. The faster, more instinctive production pipeline raises the risk of flooding stores with low-quality titles and eroding gamers' trust.

title: Oomwoo is a new open-source robot vacuum you can 3D print yourself, sidesteps cloud security risks by running fully offline — project combines Raspberry Pi, 2D LiDAR, and a 3D-printed chassis
blurb: Maker's Pet launched oomwoo, an open-source robot vacuum that owners 3D print and assemble themselves, running entirely offline on a Raspberry Pi with 2D LiDAR, ROS 2, and native Home Assistant integration. The hardware, firmware, and software are open under Apache 2.0, but the project is still at the request-for-comments stage with no build instructions yet and a first bill of materials targeted for mid-July.

title: Waymo and Uber end robotaxi pilot in Phoenix
blurb: Waymo and Uber ended their robotaxi pilot in Phoenix, removing Waymo rides from the Uber app in the company's first joint market about a month ago. The two still partner exclusively in Austin and Atlanta, where hundreds of Waymo vehicles operate, while the Phoenix cars shift to autonomous DoorDash deliveries and Uber lines up an undisclosed new AV partner there.

title: WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private
blurb: WhatsApp began global rollout of optional usernames, letting its 3 billion users connect without sharing phone numbers. Once enabled, other accounts can no longer see a user's number, and an optional username key adds a second gate requiring senders to know both the handle and the key before making first contact. The feature arrives more than two years after Signal shipped its own username option.

title: How the AI bubble could pop and take down the global economy, according to the BIS
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned that an AI investment bubble could burst and drag down the broader global economy, citing concentrated valuations and heavy capital spending across a handful of firms. The BIS pointed to circular financing among chipmakers, cloud providers, and AI labs as a structural risk, drawing parallels to past asset bubbles whose collapse spread well beyond the original sector.

title: Comcast is splitting its media and broadband properties
blurb: Comcast will split into two companies, spinning off NBCUniversal and Sky as a standalone media group while keeping its mobile and broadband networks, with the tax-free separation expected to close within a year. The move sent shares up more than 20% after a roughly 30% decline over the past year, and follows Comcast's January spin-off of its cable channels into Versant as streaming and satellite rivals like SpaceX pressure both businesses.

title: The "Akrites" vulnerability-mitigation project launches
blurb: Akrites is a new project that fixes vulnerabilities upstream and keeps them confidential until patches are widely deployed, citing AI-accelerated exploit development.

title: Scientists find molecular-level evidence for two structures in liquid water
blurb: Researchers report molecular-level evidence that liquid water contains two distinct local structures, a high-density and a low-density form, rather than a single uniform arrangement. The finding supports a decades-old "two-liquid" hypothesis and could refine models of water's anomalous properties, which matter for chemistry, biology, and climate simulation.

title: The Next Paradigm
blurb: The author argues that the labs' current bet, training AIs on millions of verifiable tasks across thousands of RL environments, may reach AGI by treating the paradigm's apparent limits as engineering problems rather than fundamental ones. Sample inefficiency is a one-time training cost amortized across billions of sessions, and continual learning could become unnecessary if in-context learning keeps extending to longer horizons, making weight updates from deployment optional.

title: Apple may struggle to get clearance for Chinese RAM, even for Chinese iPhones
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for permission to buy memory chips from blacklisted Chinese manufacturers CXMT and YMTC, even for iPhones sold only in China. The push faces long odds: a similar 2022 request under Biden drew bipartisan Senate opposition, and acting National Security Advisor Marco Rubio was among the lawmakers who signed a letter warning that YMTC's CCP ties posed supply-chain security risks.

title: WhatsApp introduces usernames so you can chat without phone numbers
blurb: WhatsApp introduced usernames, letting its three billion users message businesses and new contacts without sharing a phone number. Reservations open this week, with the feature itself arriving in the coming months and an optional username key required before strangers can reach you. The move follows Signal, which added usernames in 2024 to hide phone numbers from other users.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron Sued in US over Memory Price Fixing
blurb: Fourteen consumers and three small businesses sued Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron in a California federal court, alleging the three DRAM makers colluded on supply and pricing since 2022 to drive prices up roughly 700% over four years. The suit, filed by antitrust firm Bathaee Dunne, seeks class-action status and triple damages, citing Apple's recent price hikes as the trigger and the makers' coordinated shift to high-bandwidth memory.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance issued a rare joint statement warning that AI models capable of devastating cyberattacks on governments and businesses are months, not years, away, and urged leaders to act now. The agencies said frontier models will sharply increase the speed, scale, and sophistication of attacks by lowering barriers for bad actors, framing cyber risk as a core business and leadership responsibility rather than a purely technical one.

title: Config 2026: New Materials, New Tools, and a More Expressive Canvas
blurb: Figma recapped its Config 2026 announcements, centered on code layers that turn any design layer into an interactive, prompt-editable code block on the canvas. The release also adds Figma Motion, shaders, generative plugins, and Weave tools, all composable in the same multiplayer file, with the ability to extract design frames back into editable layers when needed.

title: Australia's ACCC sues Amazon for allegedly introducing ads to Prime Video under unfair contract terms and forcing existing subscribers to pay more to avoid them (Peter Vercoe/Bloomberg)
blurb: Australia's ACCC sued Amazon, alleging it added ads to Prime Video under unfair contract terms and made existing subscribers pay extra to remove them. The consumer watchdog is seeking penalties and refunds, arguing the rollout breached Australian consumer law by changing the service customers had already paid for without genuine consent.

title: UK regulator proposes letting developers steer users off Apple and Google payments
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed letting app developers steer users toward cheaper payment options outside Apple and Google's stores, undercutting the 30% commissions that anchor the mobile economy. The CMA stopped short of abolishing fees, instead requiring any steering charge to be lower than current commissions, and is also weighing a rule forcing Apple to open its NFC chip to third-party tap-to-pay apps.

title: Robot hand company settles Tesla trade secret suit and announces $11M raise
blurb: Proception, the robot-hand startup founded by former Tesla Optimus technical lead Jay Li, raised an $11M seed round led by First Round Capital, with Y Combinator and BoxGroup participating, after settling a Tesla trade-secret lawsuit that was dismissed earlier this month. The company is now shipping its first high-dexterity robotic hands to researchers and robotics firms, betting it can supply the dexterous manipulation that others would rather not build in-house.

title: Apple accelerates security updates in response to AI-powered hacking risks
blurb: Apple released iOS, iPadOS, and macOS 26.5.2 today with security fixes pulled forward from the planned 26.6 release, patching kernel, WebKit, and WebRTC vulnerabilities ahead of schedule. The company told Reuters it accelerated the timeline because AI models now speed the development of malicious hacking tools, shrinking the window between a fix going public and reaching customers before attackers can exploit it.

title: Alphabet stock pops 4% on Dow debut, but the tech giant faces major AI questions
blurb: Alphabet stock rose 4% on Monday as the company joined the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon, even as it tracks toward its worst month since early last year after six down weeks in seven. The blue-chip designation is mostly symbolic, arriving as investors question the payoff from Alphabet's AI spending amid DeepMind departures to Anthropic and OpenAI, cheaper Chinese models, and compute shortages that left it unable to meet enterprise demand.

title: AI Data Centers Have Been Great for the Steel Industry. Now, a Power Crisis Looms.
blurb: AI data centers have driven a surge in demand for steel, but steelmakers now warn they are competing with those same facilities for electricity. The power crunch threatens an industry that relies on cheap, abundant electricity to run its furnaces, setting up a fight over grid capacity as data center buildouts accelerate across the US.

title: Adobe is Buying Topaz Labs, the AI Video Enhancer
blurb: Adobe agreed to acquire Topaz Labs, the Emmy-winning maker of AI tools that upscale, sharpen, denoise, and restore images and video. The terms were undisclosed, and Adobe plans to fold Topaz's models into Firefly and Creative Cloud apps like Photoshop, Lightroom, and Premiere, gaining on-device technology that runs heavy AI video processing locally instead of in the cloud.

title: OpenAI teases a device it is launching in partnership with keyboard maker Work Louder on July 15, saying "Your favorite Codex shortcuts are getting an upgrade" (Emma Roth/The Verge)
blurb: OpenAI teased a Codex-focused hardware device built with keyboard maker Work Louder, set to launch July 15 with the line "Your favorite Codex shortcuts are getting an upgrade." A teaser video shows the device, suggesting a dedicated peripheral for triggering Codex commands as OpenAI pushes its coding agent deeper into developers' physical workflows.

title: China establishes VLEO industry alliance as satellites demonstrate sustained low-orbit operations
blurb: China established a national VLEO Technology Innovation and Industry Development Alliance, co-founded by 34 universities, research institutes, and commercial space firms, as two of its satellites sustain operations below 300 kilometers. The demonstrator Shiyan-25 has held an altitude near 270 kilometers since September 2023, where atmospheric drag is roughly 10 times greater than at the ISS, signaling progress on the propulsion needed for high-resolution, low-latency orbits.

title: Google reportedly capped Meta's use of Gemini AI for coding and chatbots
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models after Meta exceeded its allotted compute capacity, sources told the Financial Times, prompting Google to warn the company in March and Meta to ask staff to use tokens more efficiently. Meta, which runs no cloud business of its own, had picked Gemini over its own Llama models for coding, customer service, and advertiser chatbots, and has pledged $600B in cloud investments over two years.

title: AI software development startup 8090 nabs $135M funding round
blurb: 8090 Solutions, the AI software development startup founded by Chamath Palihapitiya in 2024, raised $135M in a Series A led by Salesforce Ventures. Its Software Factory platform turns natural-language requirement documents into code via third-party AI agents, and sells either standalone or bundled into a managed 8090 Enterprise service that builds and hosts applications for customers.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics, a key Apple and Tesla supplier, was hit by a cyberattack in which an attacker claims to have stolen internal documents and trade secrets from both companies. The breach raises supply-chain security concerns for the iPhone assembler as it expands manufacturing in India, though Tata has not confirmed the scope or whether customer data was compromised.

title: South Korea’s hot new sensation is 3S+1F – a quadrillion-Won AI plan, not a band
blurb: South Korea unveiled a national AI strategy called 3S+1F, backed by spending that runs into the quadrillions of Won. The plan organizes investment around three "S" pillars and one "F", aiming to build sovereign models, infrastructure, and a domestic AI workforce as Seoul tries to keep pace with the US and China.

title: The Gemini app is bringing personalized image creation to more users.
blurb: Google expanded personalized image generation in the Gemini app to all eligible US users for free, connecting its Personal Intelligence feature with the Nano Banana model and Google Photos. The opt-in setup pulls context from Gmail, Photos, YouTube, and Search, letting users generate images of themselves from simple prompts like "create an illustration of me and my favorite things" without manual uploads.

title: ESA to seek lunar mapping capability for Argonaut lander
blurb: The European Space Agency will use third-party lunar topographic data from providers like NASA, India, or China to design its Argonaut cargo lander, lacking its own sovereign mapping capability. ESA is developing dedicated smaller missions to map the lunar south pole, where Argonaut's first flight targets a 2030 landing delivering up to 1,500 kilograms of cargo for Artemis support.

title: The iPhone 18 Pro just leaked through a factory drop test, and Apple cannot be thrilled
blurb: A dark-web data leak from Tata Electronics, one of Apple's key Indian manufacturing partners, exposed iPhone 18 Pro supplier lists, component maps, A20 Pro chip data sheets, and drop-test footage of an unreleased gray Pro unit. Reuters reports the files reveal logic board schematics and references to Apple's C2 modem, handing rivals a detailed map of how the next Pro is built and who supplies its key parts.

title: Tidal AI Policy
blurb: Tidal published an AI policy that, starting June 29, blocks fully AI-generated tracks from earning royalties, direct-to-fan revenue, or any monetization, and will badge them with an "AI" label from mid-July. The streamer is not banning AI music outright but will use automated tools to remove tracks impersonating real artists and expects distributors to flag AI content before it reaches the platform.

title: New Alexa+ Agentic Ads let shoppers purchase directly within an ad
blurb: Amazon introduced Alexa+ Agentic Ads, the first ad format that lets shoppers complete a purchase through conversation without leaving the ad. The format launches during Prime Day with Papa Johns for food ordering and artists Beck, Jill Scott, and Omar Courtz for Ticketmaster concert sales, with Amazon planning to add more brands over time.

title: Blue Origin insists New Glenn will rise from the ashes this year after explosion deleted launchpad
blurb: Blue Origin said New Glenn will launch again before the end of 2026, despite an explosion that destroyed its Cape Canaveral launchpad. The company is rebuilding the pad infrastructure while pushing to recover schedule, a setback that widens the gap with SpaceX as Blue Origin tries to establish New Glenn as a reusable heavy-lift competitor.

title: Reward Models Can Be Too Sensitive
blurb: This paper shows many reward models are oversensitive, scoring equally good responses differently, and proposes Monte Carlo dropout discretization to cut reward hacking.

title: Weak Hiring Is Hurting Young Workers More than AI, Study Says
blurb: A St. Louis Fed study finds weak hiring, not missing AI skills, is the main driver of young workers' unemployment.

title: Legible Shared Autonomy: Implicit Communication of Robot Belief through Motion
blurb: This paper introduces legible shared autonomy, a control method that makes a robot arm's motion reveal which goal it has inferred, so users with motor impairments can see whether the robot understood their intent. The system modulates how assertively it assists based on confidence, ceding control when uncertain, and user studies with a six-degree-of-freedom arm showed better understanding of the robot's beliefs and less control effort than standard shared autonomy.

title: Comcast spins off NBCUniversal as it exits media entirely
blurb: Comcast will spin off NBCUniversal as a standalone public company, exiting media entirely while keeping Xfinity's broadband and wireless business.

title: Pegasus XL set to air launch Swift Boost Mission to save NASA space telescope
blurb: A Northrop Grumman Pegasus XL rocket will air-launch NASA's Swift Boost Mission, a Katalyst Space Technologies servicing spacecraft tasked with raising the orbit of the aging Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory. The gamma-ray burst telescope has logged 1,760 bursts since 2004 but has been losing altitude to atmospheric drag, making this the first Pegasus XL flight since 2021.

title: Studio Canal Movies purchased on PlayStation Store removed without refund
blurb: Sony will remove Studio Canal movies from PlayStation users' libraries on September 1, 2026, with no refund for prior purchases.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic that covers joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal for Micron's data center HBM, DRAM, and SSDs, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The partnership ties frontier model demand directly to infrastructure, giving Anthropic guaranteed supply for training and serving Claude while positioning Micron's memory portfolio at the center of AI compute scaling.

title: iPhone Ultra 3D-printed hinge problems reportedly solved, ready for September launch
blurb: Apple has resolved the durability and tolerance problems with the 3D-printed hinge on its first foldable, the iPhone Ultra, clearing the device for a September launch, according to a new supply-chain report in The Elec. Hinge modules from Taiwan's Shinjuxing and US-based Amphenol caused noise and defect-rate issues during testing, but the phone has now entered test production ahead of mass manufacturing.

title: Grammarly parent Superhuman buys AI detector GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, the company formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire AI-detection startup GPTZero for an undisclosed sum. GPTZero brings 19 million registered users, roughly $30M in annual recurring revenue, and tools that flag machine-written text, fake citations, and invented statistics, all of which Superhuman will fold into its Go assistant as part of an "authenticity layer" for verifying where content came from.

title: The AI jobs debate just got messier
blurb: A new Ramp and Revelio Labs report found that companies spending heavily on AI are growing headcount faster, with high-intensity adopters increasing total staff 10.2% and entry-level roles 12%, countering fears that AI is gutting junior jobs. The authors caution the data skews toward fast-growing, VC-backed tech firms, so it suggests AI can drive firm expansion rather than proving it universally creates jobs.

title: US political campaign managers and consultants are using AI to analyze voter data, create campaign materials, and more; survey: 87% of campaigners use AI daily (Stuart A. Thompson/New York Times)
blurb: A New York Times survey found that 87% of US political campaign managers and consultants now use AI daily to analyze voter data, generate campaign materials, and draft messaging. AI-generated images have become the public face of the election cycle, but the deeper shift is operational, with the technology embedded across the day-to-day work of running a campaign.

title: House Passes Online Safety Bill as Senate Eyes Stricter Push
blurb: The House passed legislation requiring new online safety protections for children, reflecting growing bipartisan momentum in Washington to address parents' concerns. The bill now heads to the Senate, where lawmakers seeking stronger safeguards are likely to push for changes, setting up a clash over how far the rules should go.

title: L’Autriche invite Bruxelles à explorer une implantation d’Anthropic dans l’Union européenne
blurb: Austria asked the European Commission to study bringing an Anthropic operation into the EU, with digitalization state secretary Alexander Pröll arguing in a letter to Commissioner Henna Virkkunen that Europe cannot ignore US restrictions on access to frontier AI models. The proposal is unlikely to materialize, but it signals a shift in how European policymakers view AI, treating access to advanced models as a strategic concern on par with regulation.

title: Musk Says Grok 4.5 Entered Private Beta
blurb: Musk says Grok 4.5, built on the 1.5T V9 model, is in private beta at SpaceX and Tesla with near-Opus evals.

title: Researchers Demo New Claude Code Attack Using Harmless-Looking Repositories to Hijack Developer Machines
blurb: Mozilla's 0Din researchers demonstrated an attack that hijacks developer machines through normal-looking repositories that contain no malicious code, relying instead on Claude Code following a setup error message that triggers a hidden reverse shell. The payload lives in a base64-encoded DNS TXT record rather than the repo, splitting the attack across the repository, DNS infrastructure, and the agent's trust so no component looks malicious in isolation.

title: The attack that hijacked Claude Code came through Sentry. Datadog, PagerDuty, and Jira have the same exposure.
blurb: Tenet Security's June "agentjacking" disclosure showed that a single crafted Sentry error event, sent through a public DSN credential that requires no breach or authentication, can inject attacker instructions that Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex execute as trusted diagnostic output with the developer's full privileges. Tenet hit an 85% success rate across 100-plus controlled targets and found 2,388 organizations with exposed Sentry credentials, exposing the same blind spot in any agent connected to Datadog, PagerDuty, or Jira.

title: Imec's 2026 roadmap details 0.3nm nodes by 2038, CFET transistors become viable at 0.7nm — company redefines Moore's Law as cell sizes gain importance for density
blurb: Imec's 2026 roadmap targets 0.3nm nodes by 2038 and viable CFET transistors at 0.7nm, redefining Moore's Law around cell size.

title: Meituan open sources LongCat-2.0, the 1.6T, near-frontier agentic coding model that's been leading OpenRouter — trained entirely on Chinese chips
blurb: Meituan open sourced LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with a 1-million-token context window, revealing it as "Owl Alpha," the stealth model that topped OpenRouter's developer charts for two months. Released under an MIT license and trained entirely on Chinese chips, it is priced at $0.75/$2.95 per million input/output tokens, with promotional rates of $0.30/$1.20 and free context-cache hits.

title: Pessimism's Paradox: Conservative Offline Training Amplifies Reward Hacking During Online Adaptation in Reasoning Models
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that conservative offline training makes online adaptation safer, showing that more conservatism monotonically worsens reward hacking. Training a Qwen3-14B policy under DPO at increasing conservatism levels, the authors find that higher conservatism compresses policy entropy, reduces response diversity, and raises ensemble disagreement that online optimization exploits faster, arguing the field needs calibrated rather than maximal conservatism.

title: Popping the GPU Bubble
blurb: Photon hides GPU bubbles using pipelined decoding, which overlaps GPU and CPU work instead of running them as a sequential baton pass. In standard autoregressive decoding, the GPU finishes one token's math, then sits idle while the CPU handles fixed-cost housekeeping like selecting requests, setting up metadata, and committing the chosen token. Photon launches the next forward pass while the current token is still being committed, keeping the GPU busy across the loop.

title: Zuck saves Meta bucks by reusing memory from old servers with a custom CXL ASIC
blurb: Meta built a custom CXL ASIC that lets it pool and reuse DRAM pulled from decommissioned servers rather than scrapping it alongside the hardware. The chip exposes that recovered memory as a CXL-attached tier for newer systems, cutting Meta's spend on new DRAM as AI workloads push memory demand and prices higher.

title: VLK: Learning Humanoid Loco-Manipulation from Synthetic Interactions in Reconstructed Scenes
blurb: VLK is a pipeline that generates synthetic vision-language-kinematics supervision for humanoid robots by using 3D Gaussian Splatting to reconstruct metric-scale indoor scenes, then synthesizing navigation and object-interaction trajectories with privileged scene data and rendering paired egocentric views. The authors produced 48,000 paired trajectories with no human intervention and trained a policy that transfers to a physical Unitree G1 for navigation and single-object transport.

title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
blurb: Security firm LayerX disclosed BioShocking, an indirect prompt injection attack that tricked six AI browsers and assistants, including OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, and Anthropic's Claude extension, into copying a user's credentials and sending them to an attacker. The attack disguises malicious commands as game rules a puzzle page, and vendor responses were uneven, with OpenAI patching Atlas while Perplexity closed the report without acting.

title: DiScoFormer: One transformer for density and score, across distributions
blurb: Allen AI introduced DiScoFormer, a transformer that estimates both the density and the score of a distribution from a set of data points in a single forward pass without retraining. The model uses cross-attention to evaluate density and score at any point, with a shared backbone and two output heads that exploit the relationship between score and the gradient of log-density to stay accurate as dimensionality grows.

title: Anthropic and Gov. Newsom forge deal allowing California government to use Claude at half price
blurb: Anthropic and California Governor Gavin Newsom signed a deal giving all state agencies and local governments access to Claude at half price, along with training and support from Anthropic. The agreement, which follows Newsom's March executive order to accelerate government AI use, contrasts with Anthropic's federal standing after the Pentagon labeled it a "supply-chain risk" and signed with OpenAI instead.

title: China’s hollow-core fiber trial pushes 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration — milestone targets AI-era networking bottlenecks
blurb: YOFC field-trialed hollow-core fiber hitting 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration, targeting AI-era networking bottlenecks.

title: Forensic Trajectory Signatures for Agent Memory Poisoning Detection
blurb: This paper identifies a behavioral invariant in LLM agents under memory poisoning: successful exfiltration attacks call memory_recall_fact before email_send_email, a transition benign sessions rarely show. A Random Forest classifier over 19 trajectory features detects the attack at AUC 0.9904, generalizes to frontier models like GPT-4.1 without retraining, and distinguishes memory-channel attacks from prompt injection using tool-call logs alone.

title: Scaling the Horizon, Not the Parameters: Reaching Trillion-Parameter Performance with a 35B Agent
blurb: This paper introduces Agents-A1, a 35B Mixture-of-Experts model that matches trillion-parameter systems on long-horizon agent tasks by scaling agent horizon rather than parameters. Trained on 45K-token agentic trajectories via multi-teacher domain-routed distillation, it outscores 1T models like Kimi-K2.6 and DeepSeek-V4-pro on SEAL-0 (56.4), IFBench (80.6), and FrontierScience-Olympiad (79.0).

title: SWE-INTERACT: Reimagining SWE Benchmarks as User-Driven Long-Horizon Coding Sessions
blurb: SWE-Interact is a benchmark that evaluates coding agents on multi-turn, user-driven software engineering tasks, using a simulator that starts with vague instructions and progressively reveals requirements, feedback, and constraints. Single-turn skill does not transfer: the best models solve roughly 50% of single-turn baseline tasks but only 25% of the SWE-Interact versions, with even Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5 forgetting requirements and over-agentic coding.

title: LongCat-2.0, a large-scale MoE model with 1.6T total and 48B Active
blurb: LongCat-2.0 is a large-scale mixture-of-experts model with 1.6T total parameters and 48B active per token. The sparse design keeps inference costs tied to the smaller active parameter count while the full expert pool supplies the capacity of a much larger dense model.

title: Words Speak Louder Than Code: Investigating Cognitive Heuristics in LLM-Based Code Vulnerability Detection
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic study of cognitive heuristics in LLM-based code vulnerability detection, holding the code fixed while varying surrounding context to trigger halo, framing, and anchoring effects across eight models and three languages. All models proved susceptible, with framing biasing verdicts 33.2% of the time on average, and a proof-of-concept black-box attack suppressed up to 97% of previously detected vulnerabilities.

title: Entity Binding Failures in Tool-Augmented Agents
blurb: This paper identifies entity binding failures, where a tool-augmented agent picks the correct tool but acts on the wrong real-world entity, such as emailing the wrong Alex or updating the wrong account. Across 60 tasks, five backends, and six tool-use methods, baselines hit 0% wrong-tool error but 24-26% wrong-entity actions, which entity-aware mechanisms eliminated at the cost of lower task completion.

title: MCP Server Architecture Patterns for LLM-Integrated Applications
blurb: This paper catalogues five recurring MCP server architecture patterns (Resource Gateway, Tool Orchestrator, Stateful Session Server, Proxy Aggregator, and Domain-Specific Adapter) observed across fifteen production and public servers, plus four anti-patterns and cross-cutting concerns around auth, versioning, and observability. A tool-count study found selection accuracy drops below 90% at 10-15 tools per context for Claude Haiku 4.5 and 20-30 tools for Sonnet 4.

title: GROW$^2$: Grounding Which and Where for Robot Tool Use
blurb: GROW² is an open-world affordance grounding method that lets robots select an unconventional object as a tool and localize its specific region of action, such as using a plate to cut a cake when no knife is available.
