It splits grounding into a semantic level, where a VLM parses the instruction and picks task-relevant parts, and a geometric level, where vision foundation models ground those parts into 3D regions from a single RGB-D image.

title: MuonSSM: Orthogonalizing State Space Models for Sequence Modeling
blurb: MuonSSM is a framework that stabilizes state space model training by conditioning the geometry of memory updates rather than the recurrent transition matrix, using a momentum-based pathway and a lightweight Newton-Schulz transformation on low-rank input injections. The authors report bounded, spectrally conditioned updates that preserve parallel scan complexity and deliver consistent accuracy and long-context gains across language, vision, and time-series benchmarks.

title: When Is a Draft Accepted? A Theory of Acceptance in Speculative Decoding
blurb: This paper develops a theory of acceptance for speculative decoding in the deterministic regimes that real systems actually use, including greedy decoding, relaxed acceptance rules, and tree-based candidate sets. The authors derive exact KL-divergence certificates and margin-based bounds for when a drafter's tokens are accepted, and show on Qwen3 models that relaxed and tree-based criteria substantially enlarge the certified acceptance region, especially on low-margin decoding steps.

title: TraceLab: Characterizing Coding Agent Workloads for LLM Serving
blurb: TraceLab releases a trace of roughly 4,300 coding-agent sessions spanning about 350,000 LLM steps and 430,000 tool calls, collected from day-to-day use of Claude Code and Codex. The analysis finds long autonomous loops, long contexts with short outputs, heavily-tailed tool calls, and high but imperfect prefix cache hit rates, pointing to serving optimizations like append-length-aware prefill and better KV-cache management.

title: SWE-Together: Evaluating Coding Agents in Interactive User Sessions
blurb: SWE-Together is a multi-turn benchmark reconstructed from real user-agent coding sessions, with 109 repository-level tasks curated from 11,260 recorded sessions and a reactive LLM user simulator that replays original intents and supplies corrective feedback. It scores agents on both final repository correctness and the number of intervention turns required, finding that stronger frontier agents reach higher success rates while needing fewer corrections.

title: The Spectrum Strikes Back: Infrared POV Attacks on Traffic Sign Classification
blurb: This paper introduces a stealthy physical adversarial attack on autonomous vehicle traffic sign classifiers using near-infrared persistence-of-vision displays that are invisible to humans but show dynamic, remotely triggered content. Real-world tests across two signs, 12 machine learning models, and distances up to 20 meters achieved high attack success rates, and the authors propose near-infrared cutoff filters and software-based detection as defenses.

title: Meituan open-sources LongCat-2.0, a 1.6T-parameter model that it says was trained on a 50K-chip cluster of domestic Chinese processors, without giving details (Reuters)
blurb: Meituan released and will open-source LongCat-2.0, a 1.6T-parameter model the company says was trained on a 50,000-chip cluster of domestic Chinese processors. Meituan disclosed no details on the chips or the training setup, leaving open how the food-delivery firm built frontier-scale infrastructure without NVIDIA hardware.

title: Import AI 463: Self-improving robots; a 10k Chinese GPU cluster; and an elegiac essay for the human era
blurb: NVIDIA's ENPIRE is a harness that lets coding agents run autonomous experiment-and-reset loops to improve real-world robot policies.

title: Super Micro Office Raided as Taiwan Expands Chip Smuggling Probe
blurb: Taiwanese authorities raided Super Micro's offices on Monday, widening a probe into the alleged smuggling of Nvidia chips into China through the company's servers. The investigation reflects intensifying enforcement of export controls meant to keep advanced AI hardware out of Chinese data centers.

title: Cursor releases iPhone and iPad app following recent acquisition by SpaceX
blurb: Cursor released its first iOS app for iPhone and iPad, letting developers launch and manage cloud coding agents from mobile following its acquisition this month by SpaceX, which includes xAI. The app supports voice conversations with agents, diff inspection, pull request merges, and Remote Control handoffs between local and cloud environments, with 75% off Composer 2.5 runs through July 5.

title: Prime Day shows how AI is changing shopping, testing Amazon’s bet against ChatGPT and others
blurb: Shoppers arriving from AI chatbots were the most likely to complete a purchase during Amazon's four-day Prime Day event, which drove a record $26.4B across U.S. retail, with Adobe reporting AI-referred visitors converted 40% more often than those from search, email, or social. The trend pressures Amazon's strategy of blocking outside assistants like ChatGPT and Perplexity while pushing its own Rufus tool and buying ads inside ChatGPT.

title: C$^{2}$R: Cross-sample Consistency Regularization Mitigates Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders
blurb: This paper introduces C²R (Cross-sample Consistency Regularization), a method that reduces feature splitting and absorption in sparse autoencoders used to interpret large language models. By penalizing the co-activation of directionally similar latents across a batch, C²R forces each semantic concept onto a single unified latent, improving interpretability while preserving reconstruction fidelity.

title: Defending Against Harmful Supervision Hidden in Benign Samples
blurb: This paper introduces Embedded Attack, a method that hides harmful QA pairs inside benign fine-tuning samples so that standard guardrails fail to flag them at the example level. To counter it, the authors propose Dual-Reference SFT, which adapts a DPO-style contrastive objective to supervised fine-tuning through token-level regularization, blocking harmful supervision that coarse data filtering misses.

title: DOPD: Dual On-policy Distillation
blurb: DOPD is an advantage-aware dual distillation method that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and privileged student policies based on their advantage gap and relative probabilities. It targets "privilege illusion," where added privileged information conflates the closable capability gap with information asymmetry that students can mimic but never replicate, and it outperforms vanilla on-policy distillation across LLM and VLM settings.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
blurb: Dropbox used DSPy to turn its LLM-as-judge evaluations into a feedback loop that improves its Dash chat agent. The team first calibrated the judges against human-labeled examples using optimization algorithms like GEPA and MIPROv2, then used those judges to optimize the agent's system prompt, cutting incomplete answers and token usage without degrading answer quality.

title: Latent Actions from Factorized Transition Effects under Agent Ambiguity
blurb: This paper introduces Observed Transition Factorization (OTF), a method that decomposes each observation transition into a sparse set of reusable primitives so that action-like latents can be formed robustly even when agent motion is mixed with distractors, camera movement, and background changes. The authors show that OTF primitives transfer zero-shot across carrier and morphology shifts, and that downstream policy learning matches or outperforms baselines under complex transition ambiguity.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot supply the computing capacity Meta wanted, the Financial Times reported, with several Google clients affected and Meta hit hardest. Meta has told staff to use AI tokens more efficiently and is shifting content-moderation and safety workloads to Muse Spark, a new internal model built under its Superintelligence Labs division.

title: AI coding agents can be tricked into installing malware via 'clean' GitHub repositories — Mozilla's 0din team shows how Claude Code can be exploited by its own helpfulness
blurb: Mozilla's 0din team showed that AI coding agents like Claude Code can be tricked into installing malware from a "clean" GitHub repository that passes security checks. The attack chains three innocuous steps, a failing setup script that prompts the agent to run an init command, a shell script that pulls an encoded payload from a domain's DNS TXT record, ultimately handing the attacker the developer's keys, sessions, and credentials.

title: Collective cooperation without individual fidelity in LLM agents
blurb: This paper tests nine open-weight LLMs as agents in a networked Prisoner's Dilemma, using the same protocol, payoffs, and network topologies as a large-scale human experiment. The selected model reproduces macro-level cooperation dynamics like early decline and later stabilization, but underestimates individual heterogeneity and produces conditional cooperation rules that diverge from humans, revealing a macro-micro dissociation that outcome-level agreement alone fails to catch.

title: Your Space is My Zone: Demystifying the Security Risks of AI-Powered Applications on Pre-Trained Model Hubs
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic security analysis of AI-powered applications hosted on platforms like Hugging Face, mapping the AI-App lifecycle to OWASP-style taxonomies to identify five threat categories and ten attack vectors. The authors built an analysis framework called Insightor and ran it across over 970,000 public AI-Apps, finding thousands leaking credentials, hundreds with input injection flaws enabling arbitrary code execution, and tens harboring embedded backdoors that suggest active exploitation.

title: One-Step Gradient Delay is Not a Barrier for Large-Scale Asynchronous Pipeline Parallel LLM Pretraining
blurb: This paper challenges the belief that asynchronous pipeline parallelism is too unstable for large-scale LLM pretraining, showing that degradation under PipeDream-2BW's one-step gradient delay depends on optimizer choice rather than being intrinsic. While AdamW degrades severely, the Muon optimizer stays robust, and an Error Feedback-inspired correction closes the gap with synchronous training on models up to 10B parameters.

title: Self-Evolving World Models for LLM Agent Planning
blurb: This paper introduces WorldEvolver, a self-evolving world model framework that improves long-horizon LLM agent planning by revising its deployment-time context while keeping the downstream agent and all model parameters frozen. It combines episodic memory for retrieval-based simulation, semantic memory that extracts heuristic rules from prediction-observation mismatches, and selective foresight that filters low-confidence predictions, achieving the highest prediction accuracy and agent success rate on ALFWorld and ScienceWorld.

title: What happens when you run a CUDA kernel?
blurb: This blog post traces a single CUDA vector-add kernel from source code all the way down to the hardware, showing what the RTX 4090 actually does to compute the result. It walks through the full nvcc compilation pipeline into PTX and SASS, then follows the launch through device files, roughly 900 ioctls, and a memory-mapped doorbell register down to the warps and back.

title: The FIL Hypothesis: Inductive Biases Help with Kernel Engineering
blurb: This paper proposes the Feedback Information Loop (FIL) hypothesis, arguing that the time a system waits for a verification signal is a critical scaling dimension that limits purely data-driven methods as feedback delays stretch from hours to weeks in scientific and physical domains. The authors validate the idea on real-world GPU kernel programming, showing that incorporating human-inspired inductive biases to constrain the solution space outperforms data-driven approaches.

title: The Fundamental Limits of Valid Transport Map Estimation
blurb: This paper formalizes the estimation of any valid transport map in a minimax framework, yielding sample complexity lower bounds for generative methods like flow matching and diffusion models whose learned objects are evaluated as transport maps. Under standard stability assumptions, the authors show that estimating any valid map is statistically as hard as estimating the optimal transport map, though they give examples where alternative maps can be learned far more accurately when those assumptions fail.

title: SemJoin: Semantic Join Optimization
blurb: SemJoin is an LLM-agent decision pipeline that optimizes semantic joins, which join two tables under a natural-language predicate, by routing each workload to the execution strategy that fits its data. An LLM advisor picks between a Cluster Join that prunes candidates via embedding clustering and a Classifier strategy for label-based predicates, beating adaptive block join by 20-33 F1 points across three datasets while using fewer tokens on two of them.

title: How Far Do On-Prem Open LLMs Get on Text-to-SQL? A Cross-Family Size x Technique Frontier on BIRD
blurb: This benchmark tests how well on-premises open-weight LLMs handle Text-to-SQL on the BIRD development split, evaluating Qwen2.5-Coder, CodeLlama-Instruct, and Llama-3.x across sizes under a matched protocol with McNemar significance testing. Model generation matters more than size and self-correction is a near-free accuracy win, while schema linking shows no benefit even at 96.5% gold-table recall and self-consistency adds just 0.13 points for roughly 5x the tokens.

title: Security researchers tricked LLMs into giving them cocaine recipes by abusing role models for prompt injection
blurb: Security researchers showed that LLMs can be jailbroken into producing prohibited instructions, including cocaine synthesis recipes, by exploiting role-play prompts that override safety guardrails. The team frames the underlying problem as unwinnable in its current form, comparing prompt-injection defense to Whac-a-Mole, since each patched exploit is quickly replaced by a new variation of the same role-model abuse.

title: Cursor releases its iOS app for vibe coding on the go
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta that turns the phone into a control surface for its coding agents, letting developers launch agents, select frontier models, inspect diffs, and merge pull requests from a phone. Available to users on paid plans and requiring iOS 26.0 or later, the app supports voice input, Remote Control for agents running on a local machine, and cloud agents that run in isolated VMs.

title: Morphing into Hybrid Attention Models
blurb: This paper formulates hybrid attention layer selection as a budget-constrained subset optimization problem and introduces FlashMorph, a method that converts Transformer layers to linear attention by jointly optimizing learnable layerwise gates on synthetic long-context retrieval data. The authors show it discovers more effective hybrid configurations than heuristic placement or layerwise scoring, preserving long-context recall and general benchmark performance while substantially cutting layer selection cost.

title: Mandol: An Agglomerative Agent Memory System for Long-Term Conversations
blurb: Mandol is an agent memory system that consolidates fragmented vector and graph storage into a unified architecture, organizing conversations into a hierarchical model of raw memories and agglomerated abstract memories represented as structured semantic graphs. On the LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmarks, it achieves the best overall accuracy among representative memory systems while delivering a 5.4x retrieval speedup and 4.8x insertion speedup under 10 QPS concurrent load.

title: Trump administration threatens 92 GW of new electricity supply with red tape
blurb: A new study from Wood Mackenzie found that Trump administration permitting changes and federal funding withdrawals threaten 92 GW of planned electricity supply, affecting more than $121 billion in energy investment. The friction lands as AI data center growth is projected to nearly triple their power use by 2035, with 7 GW of capacity already canceled on federal land in 2025.

title: On the Internet, Nobody Knows You're an LLM Bot: Unmasking Web Agents with Multi-Layer Fingerprinting
blurb: This paper deploys honeysites protected by anti-bot mechanisms like CAPTCHAs, proof-of-work, and Cloudflare, then prompts six LLM-based web agents to visit them while applying network-, HTTP-, and browser-level fingerprinting. The authors find that some agents bypass every anti-bot defense, yet all six can be distinguished from humans and from each other, and that stealth techniques often raise detectability rather than lower it.

title: Using Large Language Models as Low-Cost Statistical Estimators for Human-Response Data
blurb: This paper shows that pretrained large language models can act as low-cost statistical estimators for human-response data, proving that a well-calibrated model achieves Bayes-optimal risk for any inference depending on the data only through its conditional mean. The authors decompose the LLM's estimation error into representation bias and optimization error, bound the bias via the Pinsker inequality, and provide finite-sample concentration bounds plus a calibration protocol for replacing expensive human-subject experiments.

title: What the Saga Over Anthropic’s Mythos Tells Us About the Cyber Risks From AI
blurb: Anthropic said its Mythos AI model is so effective at finding software vulnerabilities that it has withheld general release, limiting access to roughly 200 partner organizations as of early June. The company warned that in the wrong hands the tool could help attackers steal data or disrupt critical infrastructure more easily.

title: A Multi-task Mixture of Experts Framework for Malware Classification, Packing Detection, and Family Attribution
blurb: This paper proposes a Mixture of Experts framework that jointly handles malware classification, packing detection, and family attribution, reaching a 0.9744 detection rate.

title: Learning from Mistakes: Rollout-Retrieval Lifelong Policy Learning for Autonomous Driving
blurb: This paper introduces Rollout-Retrieval Lifelong Policy Learning (R²LPL), a framework that improves autonomous driving policies by retrieving corrective targets from recoverable mistakes the policy makes in closed-loop scenarios. On the nuPlan benchmarks, a few rollout and continual-learning cycles lifted a moderately performing planner to state-of-the-art results, particularly on the long-tail Test14-hard split.

title: Field Order Should Not Matter: Permutation-Invariant Embedding Model Fine-Tuning for Structured Metadata Retrieval
blurb: This paper introduces permutation-invariant fine-tuning (PI-FT), which serializes structured metadata records under a randomly sampled field order with field dropout so embeddings bind meaning to field labels rather than absolute position. The two-line data-loader change cuts the penalty from rebuilding an index under a different field order from 7.4 to 0.2 nDCG@10 points, and a fine-tuned 118M-parameter CPU encoder beats text-embedding-3-large on the authors' 15-language DevDataBench.

title: Qwen 3.6 27B is the sweet spot for local development
blurb: Qwen 3.6 ships in two variants, a mixture-of-experts 35B A3B and a slower but stronger dense 27B, and this post argues the dense 27B is the best local model yet for general-purpose development. The author ran it with llama.cpp on an 8-bit quantization, where it built a hexagonal Minesweeper as a proper pnpm package from a single prompt.

title: COHORT: Collaborative Orchestration for Hardening via Offensive Replay on Emulated Topologies
blurb: COHORT is an end-to-end multi-agent LLM framework that automatically generates deployable network mitigations against observed adversaries, replacing weeks of manual analyst work. Role-decomposed agents propose candidates, implement them as real device commands on a GNS3 emulator running vendor firmware, and validate each via offensive replay, achieving a 46.7% success rate at disrupting attacks while preserving connectivity, 4.4 times a single-agent baseline.

title: Uncovering Salience-Driven Dynamics in Consumer Confidence with Generative Social Simulation
blurb: ConsumerSim is a generative simulation framework that reconstructs Consumer Confidence Index dynamics from a microdata-calibrated synthetic population reacting to time-stamped macroeconomic, policy, and news signals. Across U.S., EU27, and Japanese CCI series it outranks persistence, time-series, regression, and information-augmented baselines, with the largest gains around high-salience shocks and improved short-horizon prediction of housing activity.

title: South Korean tech giants commit over $550B to ease ‘ RAMageddon’
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix committed $518B to build four new memory fabs in southwestern South Korea, part of a national plan that pushes total industry spending past $900B on chips, AI data centers, and physical AI through 2035. The buildout responds to "RAMageddon," the AI-driven memory shortage now fueling record demand at Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron.

title: Arena, the AI leaderboard everyone uses, is now a $100M business
blurb: Arena, the crowdsourced AI model leaderboard that began as a UC Berkeley research project in 2023, has reached $100M in annualized run-rate revenue just eight months after launching its commercial service. Revenue comes from AI Evaluations, a service that sells model labs and enterprises performance analytics drawn from over 10 million community evaluations, up from $30M in annualized revenue this January.

title: Cursor now has a mobile app for guiding your coding agent on the go
blurb: Cursor released an iOS app that lets developers spin up new coding agents or steer agents started from the desktop client, extending the agent-centric workflow introduced in Cursor 2.0. The launch follows comparable mobile tools from Anthropic and OpenAI, reflecting a shift toward overseeing code-writing agents from a phone rather than editing code on desktop setups.

title: 14x faster embeddings: how we rebuilt the ONNX path in Manticore
blurb: Manticore Search 27.1.5 ships a new ONNX Runtime backend for Auto Embeddings that runs ~14x faster on average than the previous SentenceTransformers/Candle path, measured across a full grid of 1–32 threads and batch sizes 1–128 on the same 16-core box and weights. Throughput rises from the old path's 5–11 docs/sec to 70–230 docs/sec, with single-insert latency dropping to ~14ms from over 200ms.

title: SurrogateShield: Beyond Redaction for High-Utility, Privacy-Preserving LLM Interactions
blurb: SurrogateShield is a client-side proxy that swaps detected PII for locally generated, type-consistent surrogate values before sending queries to third-party LLM APIs, then restores the originals in the response. Its three-stage detection cascade covers 22 PII types at a 98.87% F1 score, and surrogate substitution beats placeholder redaction by 13.26 points of BERTScore while leaking no real values to a prompted LLM adversary.

title: Mag 7 value shrinks by $2.3 trillion amid AI spending jitters — but investors are still backing chipmakers
blurb: The Magnificent 7 has lost roughly $2.3 trillion in market value this month as investors scrutinize the hundreds of billions in AI infrastructure spending by Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta, with the CNBC Mag 7 Index down 10% in June. Semiconductor stocks have moved the other way, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index up around 6% this month and more than 90% on the year against a 3.4% decline for the Mag 7.

title: Beyond automation: How much does AI really cost?
blurb: Enterprise AI costs are spiraling not from high-token executive use but from quiet, high-frequency automated background tasks, with one anonymous company spending $500M in a single month on Claude after rolling it out without usage limits. The author argues operational cost is the product of three variables (tokens × frequency × users) that must be modeled per workflow before the architecture is finalized, since deployments that avoided budget surprises all did this upfront.

title: How the Senate’s AI AGENT Act could reshape enterprise AI governance
blurb: Senator Mark Warner released a discussion draft of the AI AGENT Act, which would require providers of "custodial user agents" to register with the FTC before accessing large online platforms' interfaces. Its requirement to link each agent to an authorizing user would create continuous traceability obligations, forcing CIOs and CISOs to rethink how they track agent activity and assign responsibility for automated decisions.

title: Taiwan Steps Up Probe Into AI Hardware Smuggling
blurb: Taiwanese authorities escalated their probe into the unauthorized diversion of advanced AI servers made by Super Micro Computer, part of a broader crackdown on the alleged smuggling of Nvidia chips to China. The investigation reflects growing pressure on Taiwan to police export controls as US restrictions push China toward gray-market channels for high-end AI hardware.

title: Attractor States Emerge in Multi-Turn LLM Conversations
blurb: This paper studies whether open-ended LLM debates settle into "attractor states," stable model-specific patterns of behavior that hold across topics, testing 7 models on 20 controversial topics in self-play and mixed-play dyadic debates. The authors find these attractors pull conversation partners asymmetrically, with Claude Haiku drawing other models toward traits like metacommentary while models such as GPT-4.1 nano prove especially malleable.

title: ITSPACE: Monotone Gaussian Optimal Transport Updates
blurb: ITSPACE is a proximal majorization-minimization method that optimizes the exact Bures-Wasserstein objective for aligning Gaussian covariance matrices, using closed-form square-root updates that preserve PSD structure and support rank-restricted factors. On real-world covariance-alignment benchmarks, it reaches low-gap solutions faster than Bures-Wasserstein gradient descent, other covariance-geometry methods, and entropically regularized sample-OT baselines.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
blurb: OpenAI is leaning toward delaying its IPO until next year, as advisers urge Sam Altman to slow down following volatility in SpaceX's stock. The company is weighing the move while grappling with financial challenges, and the cautious timeline reflects concerns about taking a high-burn AI business public into an uncertain market.

title: Factorizable Normalizing Flows for parameter-dependent density morphing
blurb: This paper introduces Factorizable Normalizing Flows (FNFs), which model how a probability density deforms across continuous parameters by composing a fixed reference flow with a learnable transformation that is polynomial in the parameters and factorized over them. Each parameter's effect is learned in isolation and summed at inference, letting the model scale linearly rather than sampling the combinatorially large joint space, with direct applications to unbinned likelihood fits in high energy physics.

title: REAR: Test-time Preference Realignment through Reward Decomposition
blurb: REAR is a training-free framework that extends test-time scaling to preference alignment by decomposing the reward function into question-related and preference-related components. By rescaling these terms into a realignment reward expressed as a linear combination of token-level log-probabilities, the method integrates with best-of-N sampling and tree search, and generalizes to mathematical and visual tasks under appropriate preference settings.

title: Synthesize the big picture and analyze trends with BigQuery's AI.AGG function
blurb: Google released the BigQuery AI.AGG() function in preview, letting users summarize or synthesize unstructured and multimodal data across millions of rows using natural-language instructions in a single line of SQL. Unlike row-level AI functions, AI.AGG() groups and prioritizes records at scale, enabling tasks like surfacing top feature requests in negative reviews or finding hidden inefficiencies in system logs that never throw a FATAL error.

title: Figma's design agent, now with custom tools and greater context
blurb: Figma expanded its on-canvas design agent with custom tools, richer team context, and skills that let designers prompt it to build reusable generative plugins, shader effects, and fills. Built on PropsKit so the plugins look native to Figma, the agent keeps work directly on the canvas, while classic plugins remain the route for AI services or third-party APIs.

title: We Ran 250 AI Agent Evals to Find Out if Skills Beat Docs. The Answer Is More Complicated Than We Expected
blurb: Wix ran 250 controlled evaluations comparing how AI agents perform tasks using standard docs, AI-optimized docs, and purpose-built skills. The team found that a slightly stale skill becomes a liability rather than an asset, with well-maintained documentation often outperforming the hand-written skills derived from it.

title: The Lab Mistake That Might Revolutionize Computing
blurb: IEEE Spectrum profiles a neuromorphic device, discovered through a lab error, that mimics the behavior of a biological neuron using far fewer CMOS transistors than conventional designs require. The approach targets the energy gap between GPUs, which draw up to 1,000 watts each, and the human brain, which is roughly a million times more efficient at comparable tasks.

title: Crypto exchange OKX wants AI agents to hire and pay each other
blurb: OKX launched OKX AI, a marketplace where AI agents can hire one another, settle payments autonomously using stablecoins, and build portable on-chain reputations. The marketplace opens to developers Tuesday after a closed beta with 50 service providers including CertiK, CoinAnk, and GenLayer, and reflects OKX's push beyond crypto trading toward an emerging agent economy.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture told some staff to stop using AI for basic tasks like converting PDFs to slides, citing a rapid escalation in token spend that has made costs unpredictable. The consultancy's agentic AI strategy lead said C-level executives are still questioning whether the spending delivers value, and framed the problem as one every AI-using enterprise will face, opening a services opportunity for Accenture to advise clients on it.

title: AWS raises GPU Capacity Block prices again as AI demand outstrips supply
blurb: AWS is raising prices for its EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML GPU reservation service by around 20% effective July 1, the second hike this year after a roughly 15% increase in January. An H200-based p5e.48xlarge instance that cost $34.61 per hour before January will run about $39 in most regions and $49.74 in U.S. West, as hyperscalers lock down GPU supply and demand outpaces production.

title: South Korea to spend $1T on more memory chip production and humanoid robots
blurb: South Korea's government and top tech firms committed $1 trillion to memory chip fabs, AI data centers, and humanoid robot deployment by 2028. Samsung and SK Hynix account for $585 billion of that to build new plants and double the country's DRAM output within five years, amid labor union pushback over robots entering factories.

title: Sources: AI video startup Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B pre-money valuation, more than 4x its valuation in a January 2026 round (The Information)
blurb: Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B pre-money valuation, more than 4x what the AI video startup was worth in its January 2026 round. The round would mark one of the steeper valuation jumps in the generative video space, where Higgsfield competes against rivals building text-to-video and image-to-video tools.

title: MESA: Prioritizing Vulnerable Communication Channels for Securing Multi-Agent Systems
blurb: MESA is a label-free framework that ranks which communication edges in a multi-agent system are most security-critical, without requiring any observed attack traces. It combines six graph-theoretic metrics with ablation and masking probes, and across three scenarios, eight topologies, and five open-source LLMs its rankings correlated with per-edge attack success at a mean Spearman ρ of 0.60, letting defenders monitoring the top 10% of edges intercept roughly 3x the attacks of random allocation.

title: MOPD: Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation for Capability Integration in LLM Post-Training
blurb: This paper introduces Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD), a post-training method that combines multiple domain-specialized RL teachers by distilling them into a student model on its own rollouts to eliminate exposure bias. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, MOPD beat Mix-RL, Cascade RL, Off-Policy Finetune, and Param-Merge baselines, and has been deployed in the post-training of the frontier model MiMo-V2-Flash.

title: OLIVE: View-Augmented Latent Prediction with Waveform Reconstruction for Speech SSL
blurb: OLIVE is a self-supervised speech representation framework that jointly optimizes view-augmented masked latent prediction and waveform reconstruction under a single objective. Reconstruction forces early encoder features to retain signal-level detail while masked latent prediction pushes later representations toward invariance, improving generation and speaker tasks while staying competitive on recognition and semantic benchmarks.

title: Rethinking Forgery Attacks on Semantic Watermarks in Black-Box Settings: A Geometric Distortion Perspective
blurb: This paper analyzes black-box forgery attacks on semantic watermarks in latent diffusion models through a rate-distortion lens, identifying an irreducible distortion floor caused by structural mismatches between proxy and target models that limits forged watermark fidelity. The authors characterize this distortion as structured geometric deviations, global drift and local deformation rather than random noise, and use it to build a scheme-agnostic method that detects forged samples before watermark verification.

title: Regime-Aware Peer Specialization for Robust RAG under Heterogeneous Knowledge Conflicts
blurb: RAPS-DA is a training framework that makes retrieval-augmented generation more robust when retrieved context conflicts with a model's parametric knowledge, sorting conflicts into Grounding, Arbitration, and Resistance regimes and routing each sample to a regime-matched peer specialist. The specialists exist only during training, so the deployed student needs no regime labels and beat all prompting, decoding, fine-tuning, RL, and single-teacher baselines across seven benchmarks.

title: RESOURCE2SKILL: Distilling Executable Agent Skills from Human-Created Multimodal Resources
blurb: RESOURCE2SKILL is a framework that distills multimodal human resources, including tutorial videos, repositories, and articles, into executable skills for software agents, organized as a hierarchical Skill Wiki of text, code, visual examples, and provenance. Across seven authoring domains it raised average overall score by 11.9 percentage points over no-skill agents and beat strong harness baselines in 26 of 28 model-domain cells.

title: SAKE: Software Architectural Knowledge Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
blurb: SAKE is an open-source benchmark of 2,154 expert-curated multiple-choice questions that measures how well LLMs reason about software architecture, covering quality-attribute trade-offs, design patterns, and system-level constraints across eight categories and four context-length levels. The authors evaluated 11 proprietary and open-weight models in zero-shot and five-shot settings, finding high overall accuracy but sharp competency gaps in categories central to professional practice.

title: Herdr: Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal
blurb: Herdr is an open-source terminal multiplexer for AI coding agents, organizing them into workspaces, tabs, and panes with mouse-native click, drag, and split controls. Agents keep running after the client detaches and can be reattached from another terminal, and each pane shows the agent's real terminal output rather than a GUI reinterpretation.

title: CLIP: Lightweight Cosine-Law-Based Inverted-List Pruning for IVF-Based Vector Search
blurb: This paper proposes CLIP, a cosine-law-based pruning technique for IVF vector search that supports both inter- and intra-cluster pruning, eliminating irrelevant clusters in O(1) time and filtering vector batches in logarithmic time with negligible overhead. Its variants achieve up to 78% pruning and 69% higher efficiency over static IVF baselines, while the LSM-inspired LSM-IVF design improves throughput by up to 141% over dynamic IVF baselines.

title: DAIN: Dynamic Agent-Based Interaction Network for Efficient and Collaborative Multimodal Reasoning
blurb: DAIN reframes multimodal fusion as a dynamic multi-agent process, using a context-aware Meta-Controller that sparsely activates specialized interaction agents and orchestrates compressed inter-agent communication for consensus-building. Across five benchmarks including ADNI, MIMIC-IV, and CMU-MOSI, the framework reaches state-of-the-art results with a 2.6% accuracy gain on ADNI while keeping computation low through sample-wise sparse activation.

title: Proofs of Ownership for Machine Learning Models
blurb: This paper initiates a formal study of proof of ownership for machine learning models, framing it as a game among a model owner, a thief, and a judge who must decide whether a model derives from the owner's original. Its main result is a dichotomy for black-box classifiers: under standard cryptographic assumptions, ownership is provable if and only if the concept class is not self-correctable.

title: Working With AI: A concrete example
blurb: Carson Gross, creator of htmx and hyperscript, walks through a single debugging session with Claude to illustrate what he calls the Sorcerer's Apprentice problem. Claude correctly found the root cause of a parser regression in hyperscript's `as JSON` binding, but Gross argues that leaning on AI without understanding the underlying system leaves developers unable to fix the issues it introduces. The piece is a concrete case study in AI's strengths and its risk of dulling developer judgment.

title: CORTEX: High-Quality Cross-Domain Organization of Web-Scale Corpora through Ontological Corpus Graph
blurb: CORTEX is a framework that organizes web-scale training corpora into an Ontological Corpus Graph, a three-layer structure combining a quality-refined content layer, an LLM-evolved hierarchical ontology layer, and a cross-domain alignment layer. The authors will release the codebase, a 24.14B-token refined corpus with its graph, and CortexBench, a cross-domain search-and-reasoning benchmark evaluated across eight frontier LLMs.

title: Before Thinking, Learn to Decide: Proactive Routing for Efficient Visual Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces PRP, a Proactive Routing Paradigm that decides whether to send a multimodal query to a small draft model or a large target model before any reasoning is generated, rather than after a full output. It pairs Draft Rating Learning, which gives the draft model an internal confidence estimator, with Joint Rating Learning, which predicts how well the target model will handle a query, accelerating inference across visual reasoning benchmarks without degrading accuracy.

title: Discovering Collaboration from Novelty: Random Network Distillation for Clustered Federated Learning
blurb: This paper proposes a clustering method for federated learning that uses Random Network Distillation to group similar clients before training begins. Each client trains a compact predictor on its local data and uses its prediction error as a novelty signal to estimate similarity with others, decoupling cluster assignment from the main training loop without sharing raw data or specifying the number of clusters in advance.

title: Experience Graphs: The Data Foundation for Self-Improving Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Trellis, a data foundation that treats an agent's exploration history, called an experience graph, as first-class queryable database state rather than disposable JSON checkpoints and session logs. Recasting frontier selection as a query and cross-session reuse as vector-seeded graph retrieval, the design reached target speedups roughly 10x faster at 52% lower token cost in KernelEvolve, Meta's production accelerator-kernel optimizer.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to a small group of trusted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers after an export block earlier in June over national security concerns. The revised license does not yet cover Fable 5, the weaker variant, which remains suspended while Anthropic continues negotiating with the Commerce Department to restore general access.

title: Translating Natural Language to Strategic Temporal Specifications via LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces a framework that uses LLMs to translate natural-language descriptions of strategic requirements into formal ATL/ATL* specifications for multi-agent systems, paired with a new expert-validated dataset for fine-tuning. Fine-tuned open-weight models of 3-7B parameters reach 0.84 semantic accuracy, statistically on par with the 0.86 of the strongest few-shot proprietary baseline while keeping requirements on-premises.

title: Muon learns balanced solutions in matrix factorization without slow saddle-to-saddle dynamics
blurb: This paper analyzes how the Muon optimizer behaves on matrix factorization, showing it avoids the slow saddle-to-saddle dynamics that gradient descent suffers from small initialization and instead learns all top modes of the target at the same rate. Muon also stays stable above the sharpness-set learning rate threshold, decoupling convergence from the problem's condition number and enabling a schedule that reaches near-perfect weight alignment in just two optimization steps.

title: Rehearsed Multi-Agent Live Product Demonstrations with Real-Time Voice Question Answering
blurb: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that takes a running web application and its source repository and produces a rehearsed live demo with synchronized narration and real-time voice question answering. It merges UI exploration with source-code analysis into tiered features, constrains a scripter to observed UI elements via semantic locators, and runs a pre-presentation rehearsal loop that converged to a 0.92 locator-firing rate on a 53-action workload.

title: HSAP: A Hierachical Sequence-aware Parallelism for Hybrid-Context Generative Models
blurb: HSAP is a sequence parallelism framework that correctly computes causal attention on packed hybrid-context sequences, solving the cross-contamination problem that prior approaches either ignored or fixed only by limiting parallelism. The method uses a sequence-aware algorithm with JIT-compiled NCCL communication and a hierarchical design for memory and communication overhead, outperforming existing sequence parallelism approaches across multiple metrics.

title: How the AI bubble could pop and take down the global economy, according to the BIS
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned that a burst AI investment bubble could spill over into the broader financial system and drag down the global economy. The central bank body pointed to concentrated AI-driven equity valuations and heavy capital spending as risks that could amplify a downturn if sentiment reverses.

title: OASIF: An Efficient Obfuscation-Aware Self-Improving Framework for LLM-Based Assembly Code Instruction Following and Comprehension
blurb: OASIF is a framework that helps LLMs comprehend commercial-grade obfuscated assembly code by pairing a token-efficient assembly encoder with three-phase training that ends in self-evolving reinforcement learning. On the VMISA-Bench out-of-distribution suite, it lifted Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct-14B success rates by up to 16.9 points against VMProtect, Themida, and Code Virtualizer while preserving general coding ability on HumanEval.

title: Hephaestus: Toward a Cybersecurity AI Scientist
blurb: This paper introduces the Cybersecurity AI Scientist, a framework for automating end-to-end security research that treats security events and interaction traces, rather than static assets, as its core units of study. The authors propose a modular multi-agent system coordinating problem framing, threat modeling, tool generation, controlled experimentation, and governance, evaluated through digital twins and cyber ranges instead of single benchmark scores.

title: DEEPMED Search: An Open-Source Agentic Platform for Medical Deep Research with Introspective Verification
blurb: DEEPMED Search is an open-source agentic platform for medical deep research that routes sub-queries to PubMed, web search, or local graph-based knowledge bases based on information density. It adds an introspective verification module, powered by a causal-consistent multi-agent debate framework, that validates retrieved evidence against diagnostic logic before synthesis to produce structured, citation-backed reports.

title: Optimization Dynamics Imprint Semantic Specificity in Contrastive Embedding Norms
blurb: This paper provides a theoretical framework explaining why embedding magnitudes in contrastive models trained with scale-invariant losses correlate with semantic properties like concept specificity, token frequency, and human uncertainty, even though cosine similarity discards them. By analyzing optimization dynamics, the authors derive an analytic formula showing that embedding length encodes this information as a training byproduct, yielding free calibration signals for retrieval tasks.

title: Give Your AI a Memory Layer That Survives Sessions
blurb: This deep dive builds a complete local memory layer for AI agents from an empty folder using Mem0 and Qdrant, addressing the problem that a new session wipes prior context regardless of context window size. It tests the stack against contradictory information that most demos skip, and works through the token math, storage costs, and access controls a production memory store needs before handling customer data.

title: Agent Security Meets Regulatory Reality -- A Practitioner Systematization of Autonomous-Agent Threats and Controls in Regulated Financial Systems
blurb: This paper systematizes the security of LLM agents deployed in regulated financial systems, mapping six agentic threat categories onto US and EU compliance obligations like the EU AI Act, GDPR Article 22, and FINRA's 2026 agent guidance. The authors document four architectural patterns from a production Know Your Customer deployment that cut a multi-day manual process to same-day resolution for roughly four in five cases. They conclude that securing agents under regulation is less about novel attacks than making auditability and least-privilege authorization real at production scale.

title: Linguistic Firewall: Geometry as Defense in Multi-Agent Systems Routing
blurb: This paper introduces ANTAP (Automatic Non-Textual Agent Picker), a multi-agent routing architecture that replaces self-reported descriptions and static embeddings with active capability testing, projecting each agent's measured performance into a shared semantic space for purely algebraic, non-textual routing. The approach achieves near-zero attack success rate against description-based injection attacks, compared to 67.3% for description-based routers, and cuts ASR by 20% against adaptive embedding attacks.

title: How to Govern Autonomous Agents in Enterprise AI Factories
blurb: NVIDIA published its Secure Agent Workspace Reference Design, an architecture that moves agent execution off the user's laptop, browser, or IDE and into a managed workspace where identity, credentials, network access, runtime policy, and audit are enforced consistently. The post details a phased rollout that treats a company-managed VM as the isolation boundary, making agent activity observable, bounded, and revocable before deeper runtime controls are added. The goal is governed, always-on agents that the entire employee base can use without exposing sensitive enterprise systems.

title: Exploring Differences Between Tabular Enterprise Data and Public Benchmarks
blurb: This paper analyzes how enterprise tabular data differs from public benchmarks, comparing data statistics and the performance of models like TabPFN, TabICL, and ConTextTab. The authors find that models scoring well on standard tabular benchmarks can perform poorly on real-world enterprise tables and vice versa, arguing the field needs benchmarks built with enterprise-grade characteristics.

title: SpreadsheetBench 2: Evaluating Agents on End-to-End Business Spreadsheet Workflows
blurb: SpreadsheetBench 2 is a workflow-level benchmark for spreadsheet agents spanning generation, debugging, and visualization tasks, built from authentic business data like financial reports and corporate filings across 321 tasks that average 11.8 worksheets and 593.5 cell modifications each. Across eight frontier models, the best system reached just 34.89% overall task accuracy and 12.00% on debugging, with insufficient spreadsheet inspection and incorrect target-cell selection identified as the dominant failure modes.

title: EvalSafetyGap: A Hybrid Survey and Conceptual Framework for LLM Evaluation-Safety Failures
blurb: This paper argues that LLM evaluation and AI safety share a measurement problem: benchmark, reward-model, and safety scores can climb while the latent properties they track stay unverified. It introduces EvalSafetyGap, a framework built on Goodhart's Law plus an Instability Decomposition and Alignment Trilemma, and runs a ten-model audit measuring capability, behavioral safety, and governance separately. The audit finds capability and adversarial robustness statistically unrelated (Pearson r = +0.232, p = 0.520), with the open-closed safety gap driven mainly by disclosure rather than behavioral robustness.

title: Making Multimodal LLMs Reliable Chart Data Extractors: A Benchmark and Training Framework
blurb: This paper introduces a benchmark of real-world charts without data labels to test whether multimodal LLMs can reverse-engineer accurate data tables, finding that current models reconstruct table structure reliably but fail at precise value recovery. The authors propose a training framework based on progressive, human-like chart reading that reaches state-of-the-art numerical accuracy with a 7B-parameter model.

title: AI Data Centers Have Been Great for the Steel Industry. Now, a Power Crisis Looms.
blurb: AI data center construction has driven strong demand for steel, but steelmakers now warn they are competing with those same data centers for electricity. The power crunch threatens to raise operating costs and constrain output at energy-intensive mills, turning a major source of new orders into a direct competitor for grid capacity.

title: South Korea’s hot new sensation is 3S+1F – a quadrillion-Won AI plan, not a band
blurb: South Korea unveiled a national AI strategy dubbed "3S+1F" backed by roughly one quadrillion Won in planned spending to build domestic compute, models, and chip capacity. The plan organizes investment around three "S" pillars and one "F" pillar covering sovereign infrastructure, talent, and funding as Seoul works to keep pace with US and Chinese AI efforts.

title: Google reportedly capped Meta's use of Gemini AI for coding and chatbots
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini AI models after Meta exceeded its allotted computing capacity, according to The Financial Times. Meta, which lacks its own cloud business, had turned to Gemini for customer service, advertiser chatbots, and coding because it outperformed the company's own Llama models, and has now asked employees to use tokens more efficiently.

title: Dubai- and London-based 1001, which uses AI to improve aviation, port, and energy infrastructure efficiency in the Gulf, raised $30M led by Lux Capital (Matthew Martin/Semafor)
blurb: 1001, a Dubai- and London-based startup that uses AI to improve the efficiency of aviation, port, and energy infrastructure across the Gulf, raised $30M in a round led by Lux Capital. The funding backs the company's expansion of AI systems aimed at optimizing operations for critical infrastructure in the region.

title: OpenAI teases a device it is launching in partnership with keyboard maker Work Louder on July 15, saying "Your favorite Codex shortcuts are getting an upgrade" (Emma Roth/The Verge)
blurb: OpenAI teased a hardware device built with keyboard maker Work Louder, set to launch on July 15. The company says the device will upgrade Codex shortcuts, with a preview video showing a Codex-focused peripheral aimed at developers using its coding agent.

title: The Human Creativity Benchmark
blurb: This paper introduces the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB), which evaluates creative AI by separating two signals professionals produce: convergence around shared best practices and divergence reflecting legitimate differences in taste. Across 15,000 professional judgments spanning five creative domains and three workflow phases, convergence concentrated on verifiable dimensions like technical correctness and visual hierarchy, while divergence concentrated on taste-driven dimensions like aesthetic direction, and no model excelled uniformly across all phases.

title: AI software development startup 8090 nabs $135M funding round
blurb: 8090 raised $135M in a Series A led by Salesforce Ventures, with backing from Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora, Quora co-founder Adam D'Angelo, and others. Founded in 2024 by Chamath Palihapitiya, the startup builds Software Factory, a platform that turns natural-language Requirements and Blueprints documents into code via third-party AI agents to modernize or build applications.

title: Straiker lands $64M to defend enterprise AI agents from attack
blurb: Straiker raised $64M to expand its agentic security platform, which discovers AI agents running inside enterprises, tests them for weaknesses before deployment, and monitors their behavior in production. The company's STAR Labs research arm found that 36% of successful attacks on coding agents led to remote code execution and 91% of attacks on productivity agents resulted in silent data theft.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Generation and Decision-Making Under Ambiguity
blurb: This paper presents uncertainty-aware decision-making algorithms for LLMs, applying Bayesian decision theory and risk-averse rules to AI tutoring and automatic peer review. The authors use conformal prediction to provide guarantees over tutoring strategies and review scores, finding that Bayesian methods improve output utility while risk-averse rules can degrade performance by optimizing for generic responses when ambiguity is high.

title: Wireless Backdoor Attack and Defense for Semantic Communications over Multiple Access Channel
blurb: This paper introduces a selective over-the-air backdoor attack on semantic communication systems where two transmitters share a multiple access channel to a common receiver. An adversary injects a low-power trigger waveform during training, then replays it at test time to corrupt semantic inference for one transmitter while leaving the other intact, and the authors propose a trigger-aware training defense that restores correct labels under contaminated signals.

title: An Empirical Evaluation of Prompt Injection Vulnerabilities in Large Language Models Across Multilingual and Obfuscated Attack Scenarios
blurb: This paper empirically evaluates prompt injection resistance across six frontier LLMs (DeepSeek, GPT, Gemini, Grok, Llama, and Qwen) using direct and multi-stage obfuscated attacks spanning multiple languages and character encodings. The authors find systematic vulnerabilities in every model, with non-English prompts consistently producing higher malicious compliance rates than English, exposing significant gaps in multilingual safety alignment.

title: Curvature-Weighted Gradient Diversity: A Noise Measure for Geometry-Adaptive SGD Schedules
blurb: This paper introduces Curvature-Weighted Gradient Diversity (CWGD), a geometry-aware noise measure that weights per-sample gradient diversity by the inverse square root of the Hessian to better proxy effective SGD optimization noise. The authors implement it as CWGD-Cosine using a Hutchinson-based diagonal Hessian estimator, achieving roughly 20% lower final optimization error than standard cosine annealing on quadratic objectives with negligible overhead.

title: Grounding LLM Reasoning under Incomplete Graph Evidence
blurb: This paper develops a theoretical framework for grounding LLM reasoning when the knowledge graph available is a retrieved, incomplete evidence state rather than complete ground truth. The authors show that no hard rule on observed state can reject every false trajectory while keeping every true-but-unobserved one, and characterize soft grounding as a KL-regularized deformation of the LLM prior that yields stability bounds for GraphRAG, KGQA, and constrained decoding.

title: The Gemini app is bringing personalized image creation to more users.
blurb: Google expanded personalized image generation in the Gemini app to all eligible U.S. users for free, connecting its Personal Intelligence feature with the Nano Banana model and Google Photos. The opt-in integration pulls context and actual photos from connected Google apps like Gmail and Photos, letting users generate tailored images from simple prompts like "create an illustration of me and my favorite things" without manual uploads.

title: Tidal AI Policy
blurb: Tidal will badge fully AI-generated tracks with an "AI" label and block them from earning royalties or direct-to-fan sales, with monetization cut off immediately and the full policy taking effect July 15. The streamer will also use automated tools to remove tracks that impersonate artists, joining Spotify, Apple Music, and Deezer in setting rules for AI music.

title: A Multi-Dataset Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Agents in Microservice Failure Diagnosis
blurb: This paper introduces two benchmarks, AIOps2025 and RCA100, for evaluating LLM agents on microservice failure diagnosis using a reasoning-process paradigm rather than scoring only final answers. The datasets span over 500 expert-labeled failure cases across HipsterShop and the OpenTelemetry Demo Store, graded along localization, identification, and reasoning-trace dimensions and validated through competitions with more than 6,000 teams.

title: CADENZA: Compiling Natural-Language Intent into Task-Specific Operator DAGs for Semantic Query Processing
blurb: CADENZA is a semantic query optimizer that compiles each natural-language operator into intent-specific DAGs of typed tasks, then selects an executable plan under user-set quality, latency, and cost trade-offs. It introduces task-extended relational algebra and tunes backend routing and relational thresholds with Bayesian optimization, improving SemBench quality, latency, and cost by up to +0.49, 165.7x, and 310.3x over prior systems.

title: Legible Shared Autonomy: Implicit Communication of Robot Belief through Motion
blurb: This paper introduces legible shared autonomy, a method that makes a robot arm's inferred goal visible through its motion rather than moving along ambiguous shortest paths toward an assumed target. The system modulates communication strength via confidence-aware authority allocation, acting assertively when certain and ceding control when uncertain, and user studies with a six-degree-of-freedom arm showed improved understanding of robot beliefs and reduced control effort.

title: US political campaign managers and consultants are using AI to analyze voter data, create campaign materials, and more; survey: 87% of campaigners use AI daily (Stuart A. Thompson/New York Times)
blurb: A New York Times survey found that 87% of US political campaign managers and consultants now use AI daily to analyze voter data, generate campaign materials, and run outreach. AI-generated images have become the public face of recent elections, while behind the scenes the technology is reshaping how campaigns target and message voters.

title: x402 Agentic Commerce Hits 500k Daily Transactions
blurb: Payments using the x402 protocol rose 5x in June to roughly 500k daily transactions, driven mostly by pay-per-use AI inference.

title: L’Autriche invite Bruxelles à explorer une implantation d’Anthropic dans l’Union européenne
blurb: Austria's State Secretary for Digitalization, Alexander Pröll, asked the European Commission to study establishing a strategic Anthropic presence inside the European Union. The request, sent in a letter to Commissioner Henna Virkkunen, reflects growing concern that US restrictions on the most advanced AI models could leave Europe without reliable access to frontier capabilities.

title: On the Faithfulness of Post-Hoc Concept Bottleneck Models
blurb: This paper shows that post-hoc Concept Bottleneck Models, which project deep features onto interpretable concept spaces, can pass standard accuracy tests while learning concepts that are semantically meaningless. The authors identify two failure modes, covariate shift in auxiliary data and label noise from vision-language models, and introduce metrics that measure concept faithfulness separately from predictive accuracy.

title: Sakana AI and 360 Security Launch AI Tools to Fill the Gap Anthropic's Export Ban Created
blurb: Sakana AI and 360 Security launched AI tools positioned as alternatives to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5 models, both restricted under the US export ban now in its third week. Tokyo-based Sakana released Fugu, a seven-billion-parameter orchestrator that routes tasks across external models to match Fable 5 benchmarks without training a frontier model, while Beijing's 360 Security introduced Tulongfeng, a vulnerability-discovery tool it claims rivals Mythos.

title: Anthropic and Gov. Newsom forge deal allowing California government to use Claude at half price
blurb: Anthropic struck a deal with California Governor Gavin Newsom to give every state agency and local government access to Claude at half price, along with training and support. The timing matters because the federal government has gone the other way, declaring Anthropic a "supply-chain risk" and signing with OpenAI after Anthropic refused to drop carve-outs against surveillance and autonomous weapons. Locked out of the Pentagon, Anthropic is building its public-sector beachhead at the state level instead, and California's CIO said the supply-chain designation never came up in negotiations.

title: Super Micro Office Raided as Taiwan Expands Chip Smuggling Probe
blurb: Taiwanese authorities raided Super Micro's offices on Monday, widening a probe into the alleged smuggling of Nvidia chips into China through the company's servers. The raid signals that export-control enforcement is moving past the chipmakers and onto the hardware vendors and integrators that move the silicon. For any founder building on top of restricted GPUs, supply-chain provenance is becoming a compliance liability, not just a sourcing detail, and the labs and clouds you depend on now have to prove where every chip went.

title: Crunchbase Data: Q2 Brought The Most Billion-Dollar Startup Exits Since 2021
blurb: Big startup exits are back, but the story is size, not count. Q2 2026 brought the most billion-dollar venture-backed exits since the 2021 peak, led by SpaceX's record IPO, which hit a $2.1 trillion first-day market cap and raised $75 billion, followed days later by its $60 billion acquisition of Cursor, the priciest purchase of a private startup ever. Deal counts are still well below the 2021 boom, but returns blow past it, and with Anthropic and OpenAI filing confidentially for IPOs that could test the trillion-dollar mark, the size trend has room to run.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot supply the compute Meta wanted, forcing Meta to ration AI tokens internally and accelerate its shift to an in-house model called Muse Spark. The signal is stark: Google is spending over $180B on capex this year and still renting 110,000 Nvidia GPUs from SpaceX for $920M a month, yet cannot serve a customer as large as Meta. When the company with one of the world's biggest infrastructure pools is rationing access, the AI buildout has clearly fallen behind consumption, and depending on a competitor's models is now a liability worth billions to escape.

title: Rocket Lab buys its way into the satellite big league with $8B Iridium deal
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy satellite operator Iridium for $8B, moving from launch provider into owning an orbital constellation and a recurring connectivity business. The deal vaults it from rocket vendor to vertically integrated space company, putting it in the same league as SpaceX rather than competing for launch contracts underneath it. For a sector where launch is a thin-margin commodity, the strategic read is that the money is in owning what flies, not just getting it there.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Spend $520 Billion on Chip Plants in South Korea
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix said they will spend $520B building chip plants in South Korea, after concluding their existing investments wouldn't keep pace with demand. The two largest memory-chip makers are responding to a worldwide shortage that current capacity can't cover. For founders building on AI, the constraint is moving upstream: memory and compute supply, not model quality, is increasingly what sets the ceiling on what you can ship and what it costs.

title: Rocket Lab continues buying spree by acquiring satellite company Iridium
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite operator Iridium for $54 per share, valuing it at $8B and turning the launch company into a full-stack space services player. It is at least the fifth deal in roughly a year, after Motiv, Mynaric, Geost, and a precision component maker. The prize is Iridium's in-orbit constellation and its spectrum holdings, and the move fits a broader consolidation wave that already saw Viasat take Inmarsat and Amazon buy Globalstar for $11.6B.

title: Databricks’ Co-Founder Arsalan Tavakoli: Every Software Monopoly Falls in the Next 24 Months
blurb: Databricks co-founder Arsalan Tavakoli-Shiraji says any business with a monopoly today won't have one 12 to 24 months from now, as build costs, the low end, and migrations break pricing power at once. He has unusual visibility here, with Databricks running at a $6.9B revenue run-rate, growing 80%+, and AI products alone past a $1.7B run-rate. His sharper read is that enterprises are token maxing without knowing their true AI ROI, so the opening for founders is to tie AI spend to a clear business outcome rather than just standing up agents.

title: Arena, the AI leaderboard everyone uses, is now a $100M business
blurb: Arena, the crowdsourced AI leaderboard built on more than 10 million user evaluations, hit $100M in annualized revenue just eight months after launching its commercial service. The money comes from AI Evaluations, a paid analytics product that sells labs and enterprises the post-training performance signal its free community generates, up from $30M when it raised a $150M Series A in January. It competes for the same post-training dollar as human-labeling shops like Mercor, Surge, and Scale, a market where appetite is surging as labs chase every last point of model performance.

title: Popping the GPU Bubble
blurb: GPUs often sit idle during model inference, not for lack of work but because the CPU hasn't told them what to do next yet. Moondream calls this a GPU bubble: each token's decode loop pays a fixed CPU housekeeping cost while the GPU waits. Their Photon runtime hides the gap with pipelined decoding, launching the next token's GPU work while the CPU is still committing the last one, so the forwards run back-to-back instead of taking turns.

title: Mag 7 value shrinks by $2.3 trillion amid AI spending jitters — but investors are still backing chipmakers
blurb: Roughly $2.3 trillion has been wiped off the Magnificent 7 this month as investors scrutinize the hundreds of billions Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are pouring into chips and data centers, some of it funded by debt. The market is re-rating these names from asset-light cash machines into balance-sheet-heavy AI builders, and the payoff thesis rides on next month's Q2 earnings. The money isn't leaving the trade, just moving down the stack: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index is up 6% in June and more than 90% this year while the Mag 7 slid.

title: Beyond automation: How much does AI really cost?
blurb: The hidden line item in AI budgets isn't your power users, it's the high-frequency automated tasks running quietly in the background. One enterprise spent $500M on Claude in a single month because nobody set usage limits before rollout, Uber burned its entire 2026 AI budget before midyear, and JPMorgan published a report titled "AI Token Costs Are Eating into Internet Profits." The teams that avoided the surprise modeled token cost per workflow before finalizing architecture, treating cost as tokens times frequency times users rather than a function of how sophisticated the task looks.

title: US Supreme Court Just Blew Up EU-US Data Transfers
blurb: The US Supreme Court ruled in Trump v. Slaughter that the FTC may no longer be independent, knocking out the legal foundation of the EU-US Data Privacy Framework. EU treaty law requires that data oversight in any "adequate" country be handled by an independent authority, and the European Commission leans on the "independent" FTC 259 times in the current deal. Max Schrems is now pushing the Commission to withdraw the adequacy decision, which would put every EU company outsourcing personal data to US cloud providers back in legal limbo for the third time since 2015.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Claude usage has shifted from chat conversations to long-running agentic tasks, and Anthropic's latest Economic Index rebuilt its data pipeline to track it, sampling down to the hourly level and classifying the output of each session. The clearest signal is that compute now scales with value: the tokens an output consumes rise with the estimated worth of the work, so building a website pulls far more from Claude than translating a document. Usage also tracks the rhythms of real life, with work queries falling on weekends except in the highest-paid occupations.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, the flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier, and Luna, the fastest and most cost-efficient. It is the first launch OpenAI is treating as High capability in both cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk, so general availability is gated behind a limited preview to a small set of trusted partners shared with the U.S. government. The models can find vulnerabilities and exploit fragments but cannot yet run autonomous end-to-end attacks, and the bigger signal is that frontier releases now ship under government coordination and a heavier safety stack.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture has told staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks like converting PDFs to slides, after internal meetings flagged "rapid escalation in AI token spend." The shift to consumption-based pricing, combined with employees "tokenmaxxing" to prove their AI uptake, has pushed spend to an inflection point where C-level execs are openly questioning the return. Accenture's agentic AI lead says this isn't niche, every AI-using enterprise will hit it, which is why the consultancy now plans to sell clients the service of untangling their own token bills.

title: AWS raises GPU Capacity Block prices again as AI demand outstrips supply
blurb: AWS is raising prices on its EC2 Capacity Blocks GPU reservation service by around 20%, the second hike this year after a 15% jump in January. An H200-based p5e.48xlarge instance that ran $34.61 per hour before January will cost about $39 in most regions and $49.74 in U.S. West after July 1. Hyperscalers have locked up the bulk of Nvidia production through multibillion-dollar forward orders, so enterprise buyers face longer lead times and rising bills, with compute costs likely to keep climbing as the memory shortage drags on.

title: Accel backs Pocket’s $11M raise, as the AI hardware startup turns a viral launch into $27M ARR
blurb: Pocket, an AI device that records and summarizes conversations, raised $11M led by Accel after turning a 2024 viral concept video into $27M in annualized revenue. The startup has shipped more than 35,000 units since its October 2025 launch, with monthly growth topping 50% in some months and enterprise customers like DoorDash on board. That traction stands out in a hardware category littered with high-profile flops, and the difference is a narrow, opt-in use case with HIPAA and SOC 2 compliance rather than an always-on ambient gadget chasing a vague promise.

title: Inside Rocket Lab’s $8B plan to buy its way past SpaceX
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy satellite operator Iridium for $8B, its fifth acquisition in roughly a year. The deal hands it a network already serving more than 2.5 million subscribers, licensed L-band spectrum, and government, defence, aviation, and maritime customers, assets that would take years and billions to build from scratch. The pattern is the story: Motiv, Mynaric, Geost, and now Iridium each bolt on a different layer of the space stack, turning a launch company into an integrated SpaceX-style operator by acquisition rather than by building.

title: SpaceX's $25 billion bond sale drives huge demand - and a potential headache for investors
blurb: SpaceX raised $25B in senior unsecured notes last week, pulling nearly $90B in orders just two weeks after the largest IPO in history. The catch is that owning both SPCX equity and SpaceX bonds isn't diversification, it's the same execution risk priced into two instruments, with both the equity story and the debt service riding on Starlink scaling and Starship working. Equity investors took the hint, selling the stock down more than 13% on the week against a $5B net loss and capex that more than doubled year over year.

title: Digital Realty falls 5% after taking $3.5 billion stake in Blackstone's Virginia data centers
blurb: Digital Realty bought Blackstone's stake in three Northern Virginia data centers for $3.5 billion, a portfolio valued at $7.8 billion, and its stock fell 5% on the news. The deal lands in the world's largest data center market, where 92% of North American capacity under construction is already pre-committed and vacancy is set to stay low through 2030. Hyperscalers like Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, and Google are driving roughly $700 billion in AI infrastructure capex this year, and they are increasingly funding it through private equity, private credit, and debt rather than their own balance sheets.

title: How the AI bubble could pop and take down the global economy, according to the BIS
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned that an AI bubble could burst and drag the broader economy down with it, citing the concentration of market gains and capex in a handful of tech firms. The central bankers' bank flagged that AI-driven equity valuations and surging data-center spending now sit at the center of global growth, so a correction would not stay contained to tech. For founders, the read is that a downturn in AI sentiment would tighten capital and demand well beyond the labs, even for companies with no direct AI exposure.

title: The UK CMA proposes requiring Apple and Google to relax UK developer payment "steering" rules, and Apple to open NFC; Google says it "already made the changes" (Sam Tabahriti/Reuters)
blurb: The UK's competition regulator proposed forcing Apple and Google to let developers steer UK users to cheaper payment options outside the app stores, and would require Apple to open up NFC access on the iPhone. The change targets the take rate that funds both companies' app economics, the same fee structure already under pressure in the EU and US. Apple is fighting the steering rules while Google says it has already made the changes, a split that tells you which one still has the most margin to protect.

title: AI and vibe coding fuel a surge in game releases; ATTN Economy says 181K mobile games launched in six months to May, up 118% on iOS and 73% on Android YoY (Orlando Crowcroft/Financial Times)
blurb: Mobile game releases are surging as AI and vibe coding collapse the cost of production, with ATTN Economy counting 181K new titles in the six months to May, up 118% on iOS and 73% on Android year over year. The barrier to shipping a game has dropped to near zero, so the constraint moves from building to getting noticed in a flooded store. The same report flags the risk underneath the volume: as instinctive, AI-assisted output accelerates, studios worry about losing the trust of gamers who can tell when a title was churned out.

title: Sources: AI video startup Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B pre-money valuation, more than 4x its valuation in a January 2026 round (The Information)
blurb: Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B pre-money valuation, more than 4x what investors paid in its January 2026 round. The jump in roughly five months shows how fast capital is repricing AI video, a category that barely existed as an investable thesis a year ago. For founders, the signal is that the funding bar for "AI-native and growing" now resets in months, not years, and the gap between a hot round and a stale one keeps shrinking.

title: Australia's ACCC sues Amazon for allegedly introducing ads to Prime Video under unfair contract terms and forcing existing subscribers to pay more to avoid them (Peter Vercoe/Bloomberg)
blurb: Australia's ACCC has sued Amazon for adding ads to Prime Video without consent and charging existing subscribers an extra fee to keep the service ad-free, alleging the move breached unfair contract terms. The regulator's claim is that Amazon changed the deal after customers had already paid, then monetized the downgrade. For any founder running a subscription business, it is a marker that retroactively degrading what people already bought and selling back the original experience is now drawing legal heat, not just churn.

title: UK founders signed the most investor-friendly terms in 5 years in 2025 and most of them didn’t know it
