blurb: UK VC investment jumped to £11.2 billion across 711 term sheets in 2025, up from £7.6 billion in 2024, but HSBC Innovation Banking found the terms behind those deals quietly tilted toward investors. Founders signed the most investor-friendly conditions in five years, with the control provisions, liquidation preferences, and downside protections buried in the fine print rather than the headline valuation. The lesson for founders is that a hot funding year masks tougher terms, and the number that matters most is rarely the one in the press release.

title: Nebex, a fintech startup aiming to act as a broker platform connecting US space tech suppliers, foreign governments, and investors, raised a $30M seed led by GV (Bloomberg)
blurb: Nebex raised a $30M seed led by GV to build a broker platform connecting US space tech suppliers, foreign governments, and investors. The thesis is that space hardware is becoming a tradeable category, and the missing piece is the financial plumbing to match foreign demand with American supply. GV writing the lead check signals that the smart money sees the next space opportunity in the deal layer, not the rockets, where a startup can take a cut of cross-border flows without building anything that launches.

title: Robot hand company settles Tesla trade secret suit and announces $11M raise
blurb: Proception, founded by a former Tesla Optimus technical lead, settled the trade-secret suit Tesla filed against it and raised an $11M seed led by First Round Capital, with Y Combinator and BoxGroup joining. The company is now shipping its first high-dexterity robotic hands to researchers and robotics companies, betting that most humanoid builders would rather buy dexterous manipulation than develop it. That bet sits against a hard consensus, with academics estimating human-equivalent robot hands are still a decade out, even as capital floods the rest of the robotics stack.

title: UK regulator proposes letting developers steer users off Apple and Google payments
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed letting app developers steer users to cheaper checkouts outside Apple and Google's stores, with any steering fee required to be fair, reasonable, and lower than current commissions. Once an app can tell users that payment is cheaper on the web, the 30% cut that anchors the mobile economy starts to look optional. The proposals land harder on Apple, whose model leans on a closed payment loop, and the CMA is also weighing whether to force Apple to open the NFC chip so developers can build tap-to-pay without routing through Apple Pay.

title: Dubai- and London-based 1001, which uses AI to improve aviation, port, and energy infrastructure efficiency in the Gulf, raised $30M led by Lux Capital (Matthew Martin/Semafor)
blurb: Gulf infrastructure is becoming a credible AI market, and 1001 just raised $30M led by Lux Capital to prove it. The Dubai- and London-based startup uses AI to wring efficiency out of aviation, port, and energy infrastructure across the region, the kind of heavy, unglamorous systems most software founders ignore. Lux backing a Gulf infrastructure play signals that capital is following the region's spending on physical assets, not just its sovereign-fund checks into US labs.

title: Zuckerberg Urges Meta to Explore Working With Polymarket and Kalshi
blurb: Meta is now eyeing prediction markets, with Zuckerberg pushing the company to explore partnerships with Polymarket and Kalshi. The move would plug real-money event contracts into platforms with billions of users, turning the feed into a place where people bet on outcomes rather than just argue about them. For founders in the space, the signal is that prediction markets are graduating from crypto-native niche to mainstream distribution, and the labs and platforms with the audience are the ones deciding who gets the volume.

title: AirPods Maker Luxshare Seeks Up to $3.1 Billion From Hong Kong Listing
blurb: Apple supplier Luxshare is seeking up to $3.1B in a Hong Kong listing, part of a wave of debuts that drew orders worth a combined $6B in a single Tuesday session. The capital flowing into Apple's hardware supply chain signals that the China-to-Hong-Kong listing pipeline is reopening for the contract manufacturers that frontier consumer-device demand still runs through. For founders, it is a reminder that the AI-device buildout rewards the unglamorous suppliers as much as the brands, and public markets are pricing that in.

title: AI software development startup 8090 nabs $135M funding round
blurb: 8090 raised $135M in a Series A led by Salesforce Ventures, with Chamath Palihapitiya's startup pitching a different bet on AI coding: developers write natural-language Requirements and Blueprints, and third-party agents turn those documents into tested, production-ready code. The wager is that the durable artifact is the spec, not the codebase, so shipping a new feature means editing a document rather than touching the code. If that holds, the moat for AI dev tools shifts from who generates the best code to who owns the requirements layer the agents read from.

title: Drag reduction aerospace startup Mako takes off with $28 million Series A
blurb: Mako, the Sydney startup behind a shark skin-inspired film that cuts aircraft drag, raised a $28M Series A led by Virescent Ventures, with IAG, Grok Ventures, and Skip Capital joining. The Flightfilm product reduces drag and fuel burn by roughly 4% per aircraft, tested on a US Air Force C-130J, with Delta, Vueling, and Asia Pacific carriers lined up for trials. The funding goes toward certification in Australia, Europe, and the US, plus roll-to-roll manufacturing for pre-orders as airlines hunt efficiency gains against a fuel cost shock.

title: Google’s GV bets $30M on a stock exchange for space
blurb: GV led a $30M seed round in Nebex, a startup building what it calls market infrastructure for the space economy. The platform is essentially an exchange, matching space firms selling technology, governments wanting to buy it, and investors willing to finance the deals, then taking a fee on closings it targets at $100M and up. Founded by former Axiom Space CEO Tejpaul Bhatia, Nebex is a bet that the bottleneck in space is no longer building rockets but moving money across borders tangled in export rules and national-security checks.

title: Vibe coding platform Base44 launches own model as AI startups seek defensibility
blurb: Base44 launched its own model, Base1, trained on tens of millions of user interactions, betting that owned data is the real moat for AI startups.

title: From charging EVs and drones to powering data centres: London-based Gaussion raises €24.5 million
blurb: Gaussion, a UCL spinout, raised €24.5M co-led by BGF and AlbionVC to scale its magnetic charging tech that speeds up battery charging and extends pack life without touching the underlying chemistry. The system retrofits onto any lithium-ion battery and spans automotive, aerospace, grid storage, and data centre power, with 66 patent applications across 17 families already filed. The bet is that the battery, not the chip, becomes the binding constraint on AI compute, drones, and EVs, and the companies that move the physics ceiling capture the value others assume sits in silicon.

title: Airwallex hits $11B valuation as fintech pushes into AI-run finance
blurb: Airwallex raised $320M in a Series H at an $11B valuation, up 38% in six months, and is pushing into AI-run corporate finance and agentic payments. The pitch is that its decade of regulatory groundwork pays off here: more than 85 licenses across four regions, annualized revenue up 74% to $1.3B, and over 90% of that revenue from multi-product customers. The bet is that the same settlement rails and licenses that powered cross-border payments become the infrastructure layer for the agentic economy, though CEO Jack Zhang says AI spend has made margins too volatile to go public.

title: The AI jobs debate just got messier
blurb: A new report from Ramp and Revelio Labs cuts against the layoff narrative: companies spending heavily on AI are growing headcount faster, with high-intensity adopters seeing 10.2% growth and entry-level roles up 12% at tech-forward firms. The catch is the sample skews toward fast-growing, VC-backed knowledge-work companies that were hiring anyway, so the data can't separate AI from expansion. The takeaway for founders is that AI looks less like pure labor substitution and more like a way to make core output cheaper and grow the firm around it.

title: Six Robotics lands €12 million to advance unmanned systems software with Norwegian defence partners
blurb: Six Robotics raised €12 million in seed funding led by DTCP's new €500 million defence fund to build software-defined autonomy and swarming for unmanned systems, working closely with Norway's defence research establishment. The round lands inside a fast-filling European DefenceTech category, where roughly €204.5 million in disclosed 2026 rounds has gone to drone autonomy and unmanned-systems infrastructure. The bet across all of them is the same: Europe wants to own the software that decides what its forces can do rather than depend on foreign platforms, and the capital is now chasing the autonomy layer specifically.

title: Osney Capital closes debut fund for cybersecurity startups at £60m
blurb: Osney Capital closed its debut cybersecurity fund oversubscribed at £60m, one of the largest UK debut seed funds, and has already made seven pre-seed and seed bets writing £250,000 to £2.5m cheques toward a 30-company target. The thesis is sovereign cyber capability: geopolitical tension and rising threats are pushing demand for homegrown security, and specialist capital with British Business Bank backing and NSSIF accreditation is positioning to fund it early. For founders, the signal is that closing oversubscribed in this environment means investors are still writing big checks where the security narrative is sharp.

title: UK puts £580M into Porton Down and £1.6B behind defence startups
blurb: The UK is putting £1.6 billion through 2030 into its Defence Innovation Fund to back the next major British defence company, alongside £580 million over four years for Dstl's Porton Down site. The bigger signal for founders is the £1.6B pool, which aims to identify and fund homegrown defence startups rather than route spend through incumbents. The timing tracks the global surge: in the first five months of 2026, defence-tech startups have already raised more than they did in all of 2025.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found real-world RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros in their moduli, a sparsity pattern that makes them far easier to factor, surfaced by scanning Certificate Transparency logs, TLS/SSH scans, and PGP dumps with the open-source badkeys project. The flaw traces to independent implementations failing the same way, including CompleteFTP's key generation and certificates once issued to Yahoo and Verizon, suggesting cryptanalytic attacks tuned for this failure class are worth building.

title: The attack that hijacked Claude Code came through Sentry. Datadog, PagerDuty, and Jira have the same exposure.
blurb: Tenet Security's "agentjacking" disclosure shows that a single crafted Sentry error event, sent through a public DSN that needs no authentication, can inject attacker instructions that Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex execute as trusted diagnostic output with the developer's full privileges. Tenet hit an 85% success rate across 100-plus controlled targets, found 2,388 organizations with exposed DSNs, and warns the same exposure applies to any MCP-connected source like Datadog, PagerDuty, or Jira.

title: Researchers Demo New Claude Code Attack Using Harmless-Looking Repositories to Hijack Developer Machines
blurb: Mozilla's 0Din researchers demonstrated an attack where a clean-looking repository hijacks a developer's machine through Claude Code, with no malicious code in the repo itself. A deliberate install error tells the agent to run `axiom init`, which executes a script that pulls a base64-encoded reverse shell from a DNS TXT record, splitting the payload across three systems no single tool inspects together.

title: ‘DirtyClone’ Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
blurb: JFrog published technical details and a proof of concept for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation bug that lets any local user gain root by corrupting file-backed page-cache memory through zero-copy socket buffer paths. The fix landed in v7.1-rc5, but only kernels carrying the full DirtyFrag patch chain are protected, leaving Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu systems with unprivileged user namespaces exposed.

title: What's new in Git 2.55.0?
blurb: Git 2.55.0 adds a `git history fixup <commit-id>` subcommand that stages and amends changes into an existing commit in one step, automatically rebasing any stacked branches that contain it instead of requiring an interactive autosquash rebase. The release also extends the built-in fsmonitor daemon to Linux via inotify, speeding up `git status` in large monorepos without a third-party tool like Watchman.

title: Pollen tried to remove my article and Google is assisting with it
blurb: The Pragmatic Engineer's Gergely Orosz reports that Google delisted his 2022 article on events startup Pollen's $800M collapse after receiving a bogus copyright claim, despite him being the sole copyright holder. The case shows how anyone can file a fraudulent DMCA notice to scrub critical coverage from search results, with Google honoring the takedown by default and leaving the author to appeal.

title: NixOS 26.05
blurb: NixOS 26.05 "Yarara" ships from 2842 contributors and 59703 commits, updating Nixpkgs with 20442 new packages and bumping GNOME to 50 and GCC to 15. Stage 1 initrd now defaults to systemd with the scripted implementation deprecated for removal in 26.11, and this is the final release to support x86_64-darwin following Apple's platform deprecation.

title: SWE-INTERACT: Reimagining SWE Benchmarks as User-Driven Long-Horizon Coding Sessions
blurb: SWE-Interact is a benchmark that tests coding agents on multi-turn, user-driven sessions, where a simulator starts with vague instructions and progressively reveals requirements, inspects the workspace, and pushes revisions until the task is handed off. Single-turn skill does not transfer: the best models solve roughly 50% of single-turn baseline tasks but only 25% of the interactive versions, with even Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5 hurt by over-agentic coding and forgotten requirements.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: MCP's 2026-07-28 revision makes the protocol stateless at the layer, closing off session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts while tightening authentication, with legacy versions deprecated over a 12-month window. Akamai's analysis warns the rewrite shifts risk onto implementations: predictable tracking IDs and state objects open the door to cross-tenant workflow hijacking, and new MCP HTTP headers introduce desync attacks and secret leakage if developers map API keys or PII into them.

title: OTel and mesh-derived metrics: A 2026 reference
blurb: This reference shows how Linkerd's sidecar proxy emits golden metrics for every inbound and outbound request the moment a workload is meshed, with no instrumentation, SDK calls, or image rebuilds, giving you the east-west traffic that application-layer OpenTelemetry never sees. It walks through where mesh metrics overlap with OTel and where they don't, then wires the proxy's Prometheus endpoint into an existing OTel Collector pipeline so both layers land in the same backend.

title: Meituan open sources LongCat-2.0, the 1.6T, near-frontier agentic coding model that's been leading OpenRouter — trained entirely on Chinese chips
blurb: Meituan open sourced LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter MoE coding model under an MIT license, revealing it as "Owl Alpha," the stealth model that has topped OpenRouter's developer charts for two months. The model ships a native 1M-token context window, charges nothing for context-cache hits, and was trained entirely on Chinese chips, with promotional pricing at $0.30/$1.20 per million input/output tokens.

title: Entity Binding Failures in Tool-Augmented Agents
blurb: This paper identifies "entity binding failures," where a tool-augmented agent picks the correct tool but acts on the wrong real-world entity, such as emailing the wrong Alex or updating the wrong account. Across 60 tasks and five model backends, baselines hit 0% wrong-tool error yet took wrong-entity actions in 24-26% of runs, while entity-resolution and confidence-gated binding eliminated them at the cost of lower task completion.

title: TraceLab: Characterizing Coding Agent Workloads for LLM Serving
blurb: Researchers released TraceLab, a dataset of roughly 4,300 real coding-agent sessions covering about 350,000 LLM steps and 430,000 tool calls captured from day-to-day Claude Code and Codex use. The analysis finds these workloads run long autonomous loops with long contexts but short outputs, heavily-tailed tool calls, and imperfect prefix cache hits, pointing to serving optimizations like append-length-aware prefill and better KV-cache handling around human-paced gaps.

title: MCP Server Architecture Patterns for LLM-Integrated Applications
blurb: This industry experience paper catalogues five recurring MCP server patterns observed across 15 production and public servers: Resource Gateway, Tool Orchestrator, Stateful Session Server, Proxy Aggregator, and Domain-Specific Adapter, each documented with four anti-patterns and cross-cutting auth, versioning, and observability concerns. A tool-count study found selection accuracy drops below 90% at 10 to 15 tools per context for Claude Haiku 4.5 and 20 to 30 for Sonnet 4.

title: A native graphical shell for SSH
blurb: Outer Shell is an open-source graphical shell for SSH, where each app is a minimal HTTP server exposed over a Unix domain socket rather than a localhost port, with encryption handled at the SSH layer instead of in the app. The shell provides a home screen of apps plus an API for inter-app lookups, so a text-editor app can register itself and open files double-clicked elsewhere.

title: Highlights from Git 2.55
blurb: Git 2.55 shipped with contributions from over 100 developers, headlined by `git repack --write-midx=incremental`, which writes incremental multi-pack index chains directly instead of rewriting a single MIDX over every pack. The append-only mode lets large repositories index newly created packs by adding a layer to the chain, avoiding the expensive full-file rewrite that single-MIDX maintenance forces on each small update.

title: Cursor releases iPhone and iPad app following recent acquisition by SpaceX
blurb: Cursor released its first iOS app, bringing its agentic coding tools to iPhone and iPad following SpaceX's acquisition of the company earlier this month. The app launches cloud coding agents from anywhere, supports voice conversations, diff inspection, and PR merges, with Remote Control handoffs between local and cloud agents planned next and 75% off Composer 2.5 runs through July 5.

title: SWE-Together: Evaluating Coding Agents in Interactive User Sessions
blurb: SWE-Together is a multi-turn coding-agent benchmark built from 109 repository-level tasks distilled from 11,260 real user-agent sessions, replayed through a reactive LLM user simulator that preserves the original users' intent and supplies corrective feedback when the agent stalls. It scores agents on both final repository correctness and the number of intervention turns required, and frontier agents that hit higher success rates generally needed fewer corrections.

title: WATaBoy: JIT-Ing Game Boy Instructions to WASM Beats a Native Interpreter
blurb: A developer building a Game Boy emulator for their final-year project generated WebAssembly bytecode from Game Boy instructions, exploiting the JIT compilation that JavaScriptCore applies to hot Wasm in browsers, the one path around Apple's iOS JIT ban. The JIT-to-Wasm approach stays cycle-accurate by predicting interrupts and falling back to an interpreter, and it beats a natively running interpreter on performance.

title: What happens when you run a CUDA kernel?
blurb: This post traces a single CUDA vector-add kernel from source down to the warps that execute it and back, covering how nvcc fans out to multiple compilers (cicc, ptxas, fatbinary) to produce PTX, SASS, and an embedded fatbin. The walkthrough then follows the launch through the driver stack, accounting for the tens of millions of CPU instructions, two device files, ~900 ioctls, and one memory-mapped doorbell register that turn a million float additions into a result.

title: Inside the Advisory Database and what happens when vulnerability volume breaks records
blurb: The GitHub Advisory Database published 1,560 reviewed advisories in May 2026, five times its typical output and still not enough as private vulnerability reports climbed from ~550 to over 3,000 per week. GitHub argues review times will stay longer until submitters carry more of the load by filing complete data and requesting CVEs only when they intend to publish.

title: Introducing Flink's Native S3 FileSystem: Built for Performance, Designed for Production
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3 introduces flink-s3-fs-native, a Hadoop-free S3 filesystem built specifically for Flink that replaces the two existing Hadoop-based plugins on the s3:// scheme. The native plugin supports RecoverableWriter for exactly-once sinks while keeping a fast read path, drops the hadoop-common dependency tree and AWS SDK v1, and ships as an experimental opt-in already running in production at scale.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
blurb: Dropbox used DSPy to build a two-stage feedback loop for its Dash chat agent, first calibrating LLM-as-judge evaluations against human-labeled examples, then using those tuned judges to optimize the agent's system prompt. The post walks through the judge-calibration setup and DSPy optimizers like GEPA and MIPROv2, with the result being significantly fewer incomplete answers and lower token usage without degrading answer quality.

title: Deno update streamlines creation of desktop apps
blurb: Deno Land released Deno 2.9 with an experimental deno desktop feature that compiles a script or web framework project into a single self-contained binary, running the UI in a webview and the logic in Deno via the same machinery as deno compile. The release also cuts hello-world cold start from 34ms to 17ms, holds resident memory flat near 62MB under load, and advances Node.js compatibility to version 26.

title: Qwen 3.6 27B is the sweet spot for local development
blurb: The author argues Qwen 3.6 27B is the first local model practical enough to serve as a general-purpose coding assistant, citing a hexagonal minesweeper it built correctly from a single prompt with proper pnpm packaging. The dense 27B variant runs locally via llama.cpp at Q8_0 quantization and outperforms the faster 35B A3B mixture-of-experts version on instruction-following, at the cost of speed and a hot machine.

title: NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project Pushes Weakened TLS Post-Quantum Standard
blurb: The author argues that NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project is steering the IETF toward "ietf-tls-mlkem," a post-quantum TLS standard weaker than the hybrid "ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem" alternative, swapping honest "NSA wants this" arguments for fake technical ones. After losing the working-group vote, NSA called another and is packing the room, and the post asks readers to email the TLS mailing list opposing draft-ietf-tls-mlkem-08 before 7 July 2026.

title: Cursor releases its iOS app for vibe coding on the go
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta for paid plans that turns the phone into a control surface for its coding agents, letting developers launch agents, select frontier models, inspect diffs, leave follow-up instructions, and merge pull requests from an iPhone. It supports Remote Control for agents running on a local machine and runs cloud agents in isolated VMs, so a session started at the desk can continue without keeping a laptop open.

title: WSL containers now build and run Linux workloads on Windows
blurb: Microsoft shipped WSL containers in public preview (WSL build 2.9.3), adding a wslc.exe CLI and a C/C++/C# API so developers can build, run, and debug Linux containers on Windows without third-party runtimes like Docker Desktop. The API hooks into MSBuild and CMake to fold container build and deploy steps into a project file, while Intune adds admin controls including a registry allowlist for permitted images.

title: Memory Safe Context Switching
blurb: Fil-C implements longjmp/setjmp and the ucontext coroutine APIs (getcontext, setcontext, makecontext, swapcontext) so that no misuse can corrupt the stack or escape its capability model. Restoring a returned-from or freed stack, swapping into the currently executing context, or running on a dangling stack all become clean panics instead of the unprintable, exploitable crashes those same bugs produce in ordinary C.

title: Cursor now has a mobile app for guiding your coding agent on the go
blurb: Cursor released an iOS app that lets developers spin up new coding agents or steer ones started from the desktop client, extending the agent-oriented workflow introduced in Cursor 2.0. It follows comparable mobile apps from Anthropic and OpenAI, reflecting a shift toward overseeing code-writing agents from a phone rather than editing large codebases on a multi-monitor desktop setup.

title: AI coding agents can be tricked into installing malware via 'clean' GitHub repositories — Mozilla's 0din team shows how Claude Code can be exploited by its own helpfulness
blurb: Mozilla's 0din team showed that Claude Code and similar agents can be tricked into running malware by initializing a project from a clean-looking GitHub repo, where a failing Axiom setup script nudges the agent into a "fix it" command that fetches a payload from a domain's DNS TXT record. The author's point is that the exploit weaponizes the agent's helpfulness through three layers of innocuous steps, bypassing local, remote, and built-in security checks.

title: Visual Studio Code locks down untrusted code
blurb: VS Code 1.26 ships Workspace Trust, which now opens unfamiliar project folders in Restricted Mode to block automatic code execution until the developer explicitly trusts the folder. The release also surfaces total cost across an entire chat session rather than per-turn, and lets a single Copilot agent host run multiple chats side-by-side that share the same working context.

title: Linux for the Sega MegaDrive
blurb: Linux for the Sega MegaDrive runs a real m68k kernel via a Mega EverDrive cartridge, with a QEMU fork included for testing without hardware.

title: RESOURCE2SKILL: Distilling Executable Agent Skills from Human-Created Multimodal Resources
blurb: RESOURCE2SKILL is a framework that distills tutorial videos, repositories, articles, and reference artifacts into executable skills for software agents, organizing them as a hierarchical Skill Wiki where each entry pairs structured text, code, visual examples, and provenance. Across seven authoring domains, the approach lifts average score by 11.9 percentage points over no-skill agents and beats strong harness baselines in 26 of 28 model-domain cells, with online acquisition filling gaps when coverage is thin.

title: We Ran 250 AI Agent Evals to Find Out if Skills Beat Docs. The Answer Is More Complicated Than We Expected
blurb: Wix ran 250 controlled evals comparing AI agents on standard docs, AI-optimized docs, and purpose-built skills, and found that purpose-built skills only win when kept current. A slightly stale skill underperforms the docs it was derived from, since hand-maintained skills drift the moment an API adds a required field or deprecates a method, making well-structured documentation often the better "skill.

title: Herdr: Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal
blurb: Herdr is a terminal-based multiplexer for managing multiple coding agents, using workspaces, tabs, and panes that you control with the mouse or tmux-style keybindings. Agents keep running after you detach and reattach, and unlike Electron or native GUI wrappers, you interact with each agent's real terminal process rather than a rewritten view of it.

title: SAKE: Software Architectural Knowledge Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
blurb: SAKE is a benchmark of 2,154 expert-curated multiple-choice questions that test whether LLMs can reason about software architecture, covering quality-attribute trade-offs, design patterns, and system-level constraints across eight categories and four context-length levels. Across 11 proprietary and open-weight models in zero-shot and five-shot settings, overall accuracy is high but drops sharply in categories central to professional practice, and the questions, scripts, and results ship as open source.

title: 14x faster embeddings: how we rebuilt the ONNX path in Manticore
blurb: Manticore Search 27.1.5 replaces its SentenceTransformers/Candle embedding path with an ONNX Runtime backend that runs ~14x faster on the same hardware, model, and weights, lifting throughput from 5-11 docs/sec to 70-230 docs/sec across the full thread and batch grid. The engineering log covers the two changes that mattered most, turning off intra-op spinning and parallelizing inside each call so a single client thread with batch sizes of 32-128 maximizes ingest.

title: Working With AI: A concrete example
blurb: Carson Gross walks through a hyperscript parser regression where Claude correctly found the root cause of an `as JSON` binding conflict but proposed a fix that would have papered over the real grammar ambiguity. He frames the episode as a near-miss with the Sorcerer's Apprentice problem, where leaning on AI without understanding the system leaves a maintainer unable to properly fix the issues it surfaces.

title: Apache Flink 2.3.0 Release Announcement
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3.0 expands Flink SQL with new FROM_CHANGELOG and TO_CHANGELOG process table functions that convert between append-only and dynamic changelog tables, plus a redesigned native S3 filesystem built on AWS SDK v2. The release implements 15 FLIPs, adds application-level lifecycle management for production deployments, and reworks SinkUpsertMaterializer for better performance on some SQL workloads.

title: The Shifting Line Between CSS States and JavaScript Events
blurb: This post maps the CSS pseudo-classes that behave like JavaScript event listeners, including :hover, :active, :focus, :focus-visible, and :focus-within, explaining why each tracks state rather than firing on events. It then walks through the proposed event-trigger from the Animation Triggers spec, an unsupported feature that would let CSS respond to events directly and drive animations without JavaScript.

title: Turning Scattered Data Into Queryable Segments at Scale: How Razorpay Built Its Customer Data Platform
blurb: Razorpay built an in-house Customer Data Platform that resolves identities across devices and payment instruments, then serves audience segments across 500M+ user profiles in under 30ms. The post details how the consent-native design keeps PII isolated to source systems to satisfy India's DPDPA, replacing a workflow where a custom Spark job once took 2-3 days to return a single segment.

title: No more Java refills for Intel Macs after JDK 27, says Oracle
blurb: Oracle will stop shipping Java builds for Intel-based Macs after JDK 27, pushing remaining x86 Mac developers toward Apple Silicon or third-party OpenJDK distributions. The move follows Apple's own wind-down of Intel Mac support and adds JDK 27, due in late 2026, as the last LTS release that will get official Oracle binaries for the architecture.

title: Introducing Package Proxy: Supply-Chain Safety Checks Without Client-Side Software
blurb: Thinkst released Package Proxy, a Cloudflare-deployed proxy that runs inline supply-chain safety checks for npm, pip, uv, and cargo without any client-side agent. It applies heuristics like flagging very recently published versions and packages with low install counts to catch back-doored or typo-squatted dependencies, and ships as an open-source repo you deploy into your own Cloudflare account.

title: How we built SmithDB's inverted index for full-text search
blurb: LangChain's second post on SmithDB full-text search covers how the inverted index is constructed, compacted, and queried, with construction done inline during ingestion so new runs are searchable within seconds. To speed sorting, the team interns each unique term to an integer ID so comparison cost scales with distinct terms rather than occurrences, cutting construction time roughly 2.2x in their benchmark.

title: Dissecting Apple's Sparse Image Format (ASIF)
blurb: The author reverse-engineers ASIF, the sparse disk image format Apple introduced in macOS Tahoe for its Virtualization framework, walking through the process of writing a parser from scratch. The post starts with eyeballing hexdumps and a numbered test pattern, then decodes the file magic and big-endian header fields, with research notes throughout aimed at readers new to reverse-engineering binary formats.

title: Rehearsed Multi-Agent Live Product Demonstrations with Real-Time Voice Question Answering
blurb: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that turns a running web app and its source repository into a rehearsed live product demo with synchronized narration and real-time voice question answering. The paper's contributions include a cross-modal feature representation merging UI exploration with source-code analysis, a scripter grounded to observed UI elements via semantic locators, and a rehearsal loop that hit a 0.92 locator-firing rate on a 53-action workload.

title: Kotlin improves compile-time constants
blurb: Kotlin 2.4.0, released June 3, adds experimental compile-time constant evaluation for unsigned type operations, standard library string functions like .lowercase() and .trim(), and the .name property of enum constants. The release also marks compile-time-evaluated functions with a new IntrinsicConstEvaluation annotation, generates Java 26 bytecode, and adds experimental support for the WebAssembly Component Model.

title: Uncovering Similar but Different Packages in PyPI and Potential Security Threats
blurb: This study analyzed one-third of PyPI (200K packages) and found widespread package replication, where new packages duplicate most of an existing codebase under a different maintainer. The authors identified 1,361 replicas of the top 3K projects, 256 previously unknown vulnerable replicas that detection tools miss, and seven new malicious packages that cloned popular ones with minor code injection.

title: SpreadsheetBench 2: Evaluating Agents on End-to-End Business Spreadsheet Workflows
blurb: SpreadsheetBench 2 is a workflow-level benchmark for spreadsheet agents spanning generation, debugging, and visualization, built from real financial reports and corporate filings with 321 expert-validated tasks averaging 11.8 worksheets and 593.5 cell edits each. Across eight frontier LLMs under a unified multi-turn scaffold, the best model hits just 34.89% task accuracy and 12.00% on debugging, with weak spreadsheet inspection and wrong target-cell selection cited as the dominant failure modes.

title: Give Your AI a Memory Layer That Survives Sessions
blurb: This deep dive builds a complete local memory layer for AI agents from scratch using Mem0 and Qdrant, so an agent recalls plans, user identity, and dropped instructions across new sessions instead of resetting each time. It tests the stack against the contradiction-handling case most demos skip and works through the token math, storage costs, and access controls a production memory store needs before customer data lands in it.

title: How do Execution Features Improve Statistical Fault Localization? An Empirical Study
blurb: This study tests whether adding execution features like data-flow, runtime values, and branch conditions to statistical fault localization improves on plain pass/fail line spectra. The authors extract features with EFDD across all Tests4Py subjects, train per-subject random forests, map feature importances to source lines, and fold those weights into established SFL formulas, then measure reference-patch accuracy and inspection effort with a confounder-adjusted mixed-effects model.

title: Google Antigravity agents get full context with GitLab Orbit
blurb: GitLab released Orbit, a lifecycle context graph that installs from the Google Antigravity MCP Store and gives coding agents structured access to projects, pipelines, merge requests, vulnerabilities, and source code across a GitLab instance. Orbit indexes the instance into a knowledge graph exposed through two MCP tools, query_graph and get_graph_schema, and in internal tests cut agent hallucinations by up to 45x while using up to 4.5x fewer tokens.

title: Building My Own Self-Hosted dbt Cloud
blurb: A data engineer rebuilt the most-used parts of dbt Cloud as a self-hosted stack, wiring a React/Shadcn front end and FastAPI backend over dbt Core and a self-hosted Prefect API for orchestration. The build targets the three features the author sees used most, a browser IDE, job scheduling, and run history, and starts with the hardest piece: an in-browser editor with git logic and shell-command execution.

title: Pong game recompiles its own source code every frame — winning entry at IOCCC29 was generated by a custom compiler
blurb: An IOCCC29-winning Pong recompiles its own source every frame, each run printing the C for the next frame with state baked in.

title: CachyOS June 2026 Release
blurb: CachyOS June 2026 release adds a Hyprland Noctalia desktop, DNS-over-QUIC via blocky, extended Python PGO, and GCC branch-misprediction tuning.

title: MicroAgent: Context-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Automatic Microservice Decomposition
blurb: MicroAgent splits monolith-to-microservice decomposition into five subtasks, assigning each to a specialized LLM agent fed tailored, multi-granularity context and analytical tools that enforce established design principles. Across 10 Java web applications it hit 89.2% average decomposition accuracy, beating the prior state-of-the-art method by 24.6%.

title: Vulnerability reports are arriving faster than GitHub can review them
blurb: GitHub's Advisory Database published 1,560 reviewed advisories in May 2026, its highest monthly output ever, yet still fell behind a surge of inbound reports that pushed private submissions past 3,000 a week. Review times have stretched from days to several weeks for many advisories, widening the unpatched window for projects that depend on its automated alerts across more than 1.7 million repositories.

title: Kafka Share Groups - Pathological Fetch Waits with Record_limit
blurb: The author traces why Kafka share groups with share.acquire.mode=record_limit and fewer consumers than partitions consistently stalled out his Dimster throughput tests, isolating the cause to round-robin fetching that polls one broker at a time rather than concurrently. The post shows how this fetch pattern, combined with partition skew, produces pathological wait times that block lag drains, with batch_optimized avoiding the problem by fetching brokers in parallel.

title: What can you confidently guarantee about your software?
blurb: The author argues that most engineers can name what their software is supposed to do but rarely state what it actually guarantees under failure, load, or malformed input. The piece pushes teams to write down explicit, testable invariants rather than relying on informal confidence, since unstated assumptions are where production incidents originate.

title: Introducing Flink's Native S3 FileSystem: Built for Performance, Designed for Production
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3 introduced flink-s3-fs-native, a Hadoop-free S3 filesystem built specifically for Flink that ships as an experimental opt-in plugin and supports both RecoverableWriter for exactly-once sinks and a fast read path for checkpointing. The native plugin ends the long-standing choice between the Hadoop and Presto plugins, drops the hadoop-common dependency tree and AWS SDK v1, and delivers measurable performance gains already running in production at scale.

title: Mandol: An Agglomerative Agent Memory System for Long-Term Conversations
blurb: This paper introduces Mandol, an agent memory system that replaces the usual mix of separate vector and graph databases with a single memory-native store, fusing key-value, vector, and graph structures behind unified hybrid retrieval operators. By keeping LLMs out of the retrieval path and adding query-adaptive routing, denoising, and token-constrained context generation, it cuts cross-database I/O. The authors report best overall accuracy on LoCoMo and LongMemEval, plus a 5.4x retrieval and 4.8x insertion speedup under 10 QPS on consumer hardware.

title: Synthesize the big picture and analyze trends with BigQuery's AI.AGG function
blurb: BigQuery's new AI.AGG() function, now in preview, lets you summarize or synthesize across millions of rows of unstructured or multimodal data using natural-language instructions inside a single line of SQL. It targets aggregate questions that per-row AI functions can't answer, like surfacing the top feature requests across negative reviews or clustering Spark logs to find memory thrashing and clock drift before anything throws a FATAL error.

title: Historical memory prices 1960-2026
blurb: An interactive Stanford dataset charting DRAM, NAND, and HBM prices per gigabyte from 1960 to 2026, with downloadable raw data.

title: Scaling Network Analysis for Fraud Prevention with BigQuery Graph
blurb: Curve, a UK financial super-app, moved its fraud network analysis to BigQuery Graph, using native GQL to run multi-hop queries across millions of users and tens of millions of connections without standing up a separate graph database. Keeping the data in BigQuery avoided a migration to a dedicated graph store, cutting cost while surfacing the shared-device and shared-card links that organized fraud rings use to evade per-transaction models.

title: CLIP: Lightweight Cosine-Law-Based Inverted-List Pruning for IVF-Based Vector Search
blurb: CLIP is a pruning technique for IVF-based vector search that cuts the cluster and vector scans those indexes normally do exhaustively, eliminating irrelevant clusters in O(1) time and filtering vector batches in logarithmic time using cosine-law lower bounds. The paper reports up to 78% pruning and 69% higher query efficiency over static IVF baselines, plus an LSM-inspired variant that defers index maintenance to background compaction for 141% higher throughput on dynamic workloads.

title: Field Order Should Not Matter: Permutation-Invariant Embedding Model Fine-Tuning for Structured Metadata Retrieval
blurb: This paper shows that the field order chosen when serializing structured records into a string silently controls retrieval quality. A standard fine-tune loses 7.4 nDCG@10 points when the index is rebuilt under a different order, because the encoder reads position instead of field labels. The authors propose permutation-invariant fine-tuning, a two-line data-loader change that samples a fresh field order with random dropout per record, cutting the penalty to 0.2 points.

title: SemJoin: Semantic Join Optimization
blurb: SemJoin tackles the cost problem in semantic joins, where matching two tables on a natural-language predicate naively requires O(M×N) LLM calls. The paper proposes an LLM advisor that routes each join to one of two strategies: a Cluster Join that prunes candidates via embedding clustering, or a Classifier for predicates reducible to a shared label set. Across IMDb reviews, email contradictions, and Stack Overflow tags, the routing beats adaptive block join by 20-33 F1 points at lower token cost.

title: CORTEX: High-Quality Cross-Domain Organization of Web-Scale Corpora through Ontological Corpus Graph
blurb: This paper introduces Cortex, a framework that organizes web-scale training corpora into an Ontological Corpus Graph rather than the flat document collections most pipelines produce. The three-layer structure pairs a quality-refined content layer with an LLM-built hierarchical ontology and a cross-domain alignment layer, letting teams associate data across domains at any taxonomic resolution. The authors are releasing the code, a 24.14B-token refined corpus with its graph, and CortexBench, a cross-domain search-and-reasoning benchmark tested on eight frontier LLMs.

title: How Far Do On-Prem Open LLMs Get on Text-to-SQL? A Cross-Family Size x Technique Frontier on BIRD
blurb:
This paper benchmarks on-prem open-weight LLMs on Text-to-SQL using BIRD's dev split, testing Qwen2.5-Coder, CodeLlama, and Llama-3.x under one matched protocol with McNemar significance tests. Model generation beats raw size: Qwen2.5-Coder 7B scores 39.1 vs CodeLlama's 20.9, while Llama-3.3-70B hits 49.2. Self-correction is a near-free accuracy win, but schema linking does nothing even with 96.5% gold-table recall, and self-consistency adds 0.13 points for roughly 5x the tokens.

(78 words)

title: Experience Graphs: The Data Foundation for Self-Improving Agents
blurb: This paper argues that long-horizon agents produce a structured "experience graph" of artifacts, tool outputs, rewards, and causal lineage that most frameworks throw away as JSON checkpoints and session logs. The authors propose Trellis, a database that treats that graph as queryable, governed state, recasting frontier selection as a query, cross-session reuse as vector-seeded graph retrieval, and training-data extraction as a materialized view. In Meta's KernelEvolve kernel optimizer, that reuse hit target speedups roughly 10x faster at 52% lower token cost.

title: Exploring Differences Between Tabular Enterprise Data and Public Benchmarks
blurb: This paper analyzes how enterprise tabular data differs from the public benchmarks that tabular ML models are tuned against, profiling data statistics and testing models like TabPFN, TabICL, and ConTextTab. The authors find enterprise tables diverge sharply from standard benchmarks, and a model that tops benchmark leaderboards can fail on real business data and vice versa. The takeaway is that benchmark rankings are a weak guide to production performance, and the field needs benchmarks built from enterprise-grade characteristics.

title: MaDI-Bench: An End-to-End Data Integration Benchmark
blurb: MaDI-Bench is the first benchmark to evaluate data integration end-to-end across the full pipeline of schema matching, value normalization, entity blocking, entity matching, and data fusion, rather than scoring each step in isolation. The authors validate it against human-engineered, best-of-breed, and LLM-based pipelines, and include a method for generating task variants to slow benchmark saturation as systems improve. It measures both step-wise and end-to-end performance, with all artifacts public.

title: Making Multimodal LLMs Reliable Chart Data Extractors: A Benchmark and Training Framework
blurb: This paper benchmarks how well multimodal LLMs reverse-engineer data tables from chart images without visible value labels, and finds they reconstruct table structure reliably but miss on precise numeric values. The authors then train a model using a progressive, human-like chart-reading process, reaching state-of-the-art numerical accuracy with just a 7B-parameter model and supporting mixed-initiative extraction workflows. For data teams, it points to a cheaper path for turning published charts back into queryable tables.

title: CADENZA: Compiling Natural-Language Intent into Task-Specific Operator DAGs for Semantic Query Processing
blurb: CADENZA is a research engine that compiles each natural-language semantic operator into a plan space of typed task DAGs, then selects an executable plan under user-specified quality, latency, and cost trade-offs. Its task-extended relational algebra exposes each operator's intermediate outputs as a relational object the optimizer can filter, reorder, route, and threshold, with Bayesian optimization tuning backend routing and relational cutpoints jointly. On SemBench, the authors report up to 165.7x lower latency and 310.3x lower cost versus prior semantic-query optimizers.

title: Apple just acquired the app that won last year’s ‘Innovation’ Apple Design Award
blurb: Apple acquired Play, the SwiftUI prototyping tool that won the 2025 Apple Design Award for Innovation, with the app pulled from the App Store and its team offered employment at Apple. The likely path is integration into Xcode or Apple's free developer resources, narrowing the gap between design prototyping and shipped SwiftUI code while removing a standalone tool designers had relied on.

title: The Shifting Line Between CSS States and JavaScript Events
blurb: CSS keeps absorbing behavior that once required JavaScript event listeners, and this walkthrough maps the pseudo-classes that now track interaction states: :hover, :active, :focus-visible, :focus-within, and :has(). It also covers the proposed event-trigger from the Animation Triggers spec, an unsupported feature that would let CSS respond to events directly, with a useful clarification that pseudo-classes match states rather than events even when they feel like listeners.

title: A Teatro and Balcony Studio tuck Museum Tower House into steep site in Los Angeles
blurb: A Teatro and Balcony Studio completed Museum Tower House, a slender vertical residence wedged into a steep hillside lot in LA's Mt Washington neighborhood. The facades use striated plaster, and interior rooms are sequenced to unfold like museum galleries, with a two-car garage anchoring the single-family home to its grade.

title: No, you haven't accidentally downloaded a banking app – that really is the new Winter Olympics 2030 logo
blurb: Alpes 2030 revealed the Winter Olympics identity, a mark of radiant lines converging into an alpine peak rendered in ice-blue and alpenglow pink gradients, with the Paralympic version inverting the same shape into a connected system. The execution is clean and scales from venue signage to avatars, though its geometric minimalism reads closer to a fintech brand than a winter sports event.

title: Popular design trends that destroy conversion
blurb: The author argues that abstract hero visuals (the ubiquitous gradient blob with a light-leak) have become a herd-mentality default that tells visitors nothing about the product and tanks conversion. Since buyers decide in under two seconds, the piece makes the case for hero creative that shows the actual product over on-trend abstraction.

title: Rick Owens creates Adidas tracksuits that double as personal airconditioning
blurb: Rick Owens debuted inflatable Tyvek Adidas tracksuits at his SS27 Paris menswear show, using a fan built into each garment's lining and an ice vest underneath to create what he calls a "personal air conditioning system." The pieces adapt Japanese fan-jacket cooling tech that Adidas is also fitting to F1 drivers and 2026 World Cup players, blown up here to deliberately Michelin-Man proportions during the city's record heatwave.

title: Koto reframes Stack Overflow around the one thing AI can't replace: its community
blurb: Koto rebranded Stack Overflow around its community as the trustworthy human layer beneath AI, repositioning the 18-year-old Q&A site from a place to ask questions to "the world's most vital source for technologists." The pivot leans on Stack Overflow's 83 million human-checked questions and answers, a direct answer to declining traffic since ChatGPT arrived and a bet that verified knowledge outlasts fast machine-generated ones.

title: Figma just made your design system debt everyone’s problem. Now use it.
blurb: The author argues that every major thing Figma shipped in 2026 (its native AI agent, MCP-connected third-party agents, Code Connect, Make's pull requests, and Config's Motion and slot guardrails) reads from your design system, so each one fails in public when that system is rotting. The piece's useful turn is reframing maintenance debt as a now-shared cost, since the people with budget authority finally feel the breakage instead of just the maintainer.

title: How I blend 3D and 3D effects to create video game key art art using Blender and Photoshop
blurb: Darek Zabrocki breaks down how he created key art for the steampunk tactics game Veil of Ashes, blocking the scene in Blender from supplied 3D tank and aircraft assets, then handling VFX with cheap 2D image planes for explosions and dust before rendering high-res passes. He finishes in Photoshop with edge brushstrokes and painterly touches that soften the 3D look into something hand-crafted.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers analyzing badkeys data found a class of weak RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros, including certificates issued to Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP (RSA keys from v10.0.0-12.0.0, DSA keys from v10.0.0-23.0.4). The sparse moduli are factorable, and the recurrence of the same failure across independent implementations suggests more vulnerable products remain undiscovered and worth scanning for.

title: Researchers Demo New Claude Code Attack Using Harmless-Looking Repositories to Hijack Developer Machines
blurb: Mozilla's 0Din researchers demonstrated an indirect prompt-injection attack against Claude Code in which a benign-looking repository's setup notes trigger an install error whose message instructs the agent to run a recovery command that executes setup.sh, which fetches a base64-encoded payload from a DNS TXT record and spawns a reverse shell. The payload never lives in the repo and leaves no plaintext reverse-shell signature, so static analysis, network monitoring, and the agent each see only benign activity while attackers exfiltrate credentials and deploy persistent backdoors.

title: Critical SimpleHelp flaw exploited to deploy new stealer malware
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication-bypass in SimpleHelp's OIDC configuration that lets unauthenticated users create privileged technician accounts, to push the TaskWeaver loader and the previously undocumented Djinn Stealer across Windows, macOS, and Linux. Roughly 1,000 internet-exposed SimpleHelp servers ran the vulnerable config at disclosure, so MSPs and IT teams running RMM on the edge should patch immediately and rotate developer, cloud, Git, SSH, and infrastructure credentials.

title: ‘DirtyClone’ Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
blurb: JFrog published technical details and a PoC for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation flaw in the networking stack's socket-buffer handling that lets any local user gain root. The bug is a variant of the DirtyFrag/Fragnesia family affecting Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu with unprivileged user namespaces enabled; only kernels updated to v7.1-rc5 with the complete fix chain are protected, so partially patched LTS branches remain exposed.

title: The attack that hijacked Claude Code came through Sentry. Datadog, PagerDuty, and Jira have the same exposure.
blurb: Tenet Security's "agentjacking" disclosure shows a single crafted Sentry error event, sent via a public DSN that needs no authentication, can inject attacker instructions that Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex execute with developer privileges, succeeding in 85% of 100-plus controlled tests against the 2,388 orgs with exposed DSNs. The same exposure hits any agent pulling from Datadog, PagerDuty, or Jira, so the fix is restricting what shell-capable agents do with returned data, not revoking the intentionally public DSN.

title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
blurb: Researchers at LayerX disclosed BioShocking, an indirect prompt-injection technique that frames a malicious web page as a game and coaxes agentic AI browsers into exfiltrating user credentials, defeating OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, Anthropic's Claude extension, and three others in testing. Vendor response was uneven: OpenAI patched Atlas, Perplexity closed the report without action, Anthropic's fix reportedly failed, and Fellou, Genspark, and Sigma did not respond.

title: Hackers now exploit critical Oracle E-Business flaw in attacks
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-46817 (CVSS 9.8), an unauthenticated, low-complexity RCE in the File Transmission component of Oracle E-Business Suite's Payments product that allows full system takeover over HTTP. Oracle patched it in its May 2026 Critical Patch Update, but Shadowserver tracks over 450 exposed EBS instances, so operators should apply the fix immediately and hunt for compromise.

title: Apple Patches 30+ iOS, macOS, Safari Flaws, Including AI-Discovered WebKit Bugs
blurb: Apple released security updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS Tahoe, and Safari 26.5.2, patching over 30 flaws including four WebKit memory-corruption and use-after-free bugs (CVE-2026-43707, -43716, -43745, -43715) found with AI tools from OpenAI Codex and Anthropic Claude. None are reported as actively exploited, but Apple says it shipped fixes earlier than usual to shrink the discovery-to-weaponization window as AI accelerates exploit development.

title: Insurance Regulators Group NAIC Hit in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack
blurb: NAIC confirmed attackers exploited an Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day (CVE-2026-35273, unauthenticated RCE) to access its systems on June 11, exfiltrating statutory financial reporting data, credit rating agency records, and outdated logs and configuration files, though it says PII and payment data were untouched. ShinyHunters is behind the broader campaign against 100-plus organizations and listed NAIC on its leak site, making the insurance regulator the first victim to publicly confirm compromised data.

title: JSP webshells being dropped on unpatched PTC Windchill instances
blurb: CVE-2026-12569, an improper input validation flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM enabling unauthenticated remote code execution, is being exploited in the wild to drop JSP webshells, and CISA has added it to its KEV catalog. PTC shipped a patch on June 18 and is updating its advisory with indicators of compromise, so organizations running either PLM platform should patch and hunt for IOCs.

title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CVSS 8.6) that chains to unauthenticated root RCE, with attacks starting less than 24 hours after SSD Secure Disclosure published PoC and a full exploit chain. Cisco patched the bug on June 3; the WebDialer service is off by default, so prioritize patching and disabling WebDialer on any internet-exposed CUCM or Unified CM SME deployments.

title: CISA: Windows BlueHammer flaw now exploited by ransomware gangs
blurb: CISA flagged BlueHammer (CVE-2026-33825), a Microsoft Defender privilege-escalation flaw, as now exploited by ransomware gangs, escalating from earlier zero-day abuse that surfaced after a researcher leaked PoC code in April. Local attackers can read the SAM database to grab local password hashes and pivot to SYSTEM; Microsoft patched it on April 14, so unpatched Windows hosts should be remediated and local credentials rotated immediately.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions tied to a single threat actor it tracks as StegoAd, which used steganography to hide payloads inside PNG, WebP, and WOFF2 image and font files, then activated days post-install to steal credentials and run ad fraud. Active since at least 2021, the extensions posed as ad blockers, VPNs, and translators across an install base of up to 2.6 million, using server-side validation, DevTools detection, and execution gates to evade analysis.

title: Oracle E-Business Suite Flaw CVE-2026-46817 Actively Exploited in the Wild
blurb: Oracle E-Business Suite is under active exploitation via CVE-2026-46817 (CVSS 9.8), an improper-privilege-management and authentication flaw in Oracle Payments that lets an unauthenticated attacker take over instances over HTTP. Oracle patched it in last month's Critical Patch Update for versions 12.2.3 through 12.2.15, and Defused Cyber observed exploitation on its honeypots over the weekend despite no public PoC, so patch immediately.

title: U.S. offers $10 million for hackers targeting WhatsApp, Signal users
blurb: The U.S. State Department is offering up to $10M through its Rewards for Justice program for information identifying members of UNC5792 and UNC4221, two groups tied to Russia's FSB and military intelligence. The actors run phishing campaigns against Signal and WhatsApp accounts of U.S. and NATO officials, impersonating Signal support to steal backup recovery keys and compromising thousands of accounts without breaking the platforms' encryption.

title: AirDrop and Quick Share vulnerabilities affect protocols on five billion devices as fixes begin
blurb: Researchers at CISPA disclosed six vulnerabilities in Apple AirDrop and Google/Samsung Quick Share spanning macOS, iOS, Android, and Windows, affecting the proximity file-sharing protocols on more than five billion active devices. A proximity attacker within wireless range can trigger pre-authentication crashes in Apple's sharingd daemon, taking down AirDrop, AirPlay, Handoff, Universal Clipboard, and Continuity Camera together, and a looped request holds the service down; fixes are rolling out.

title: Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks
blurb: Mustang Panda is running two espionage campaigns against Indian government and hydropower targets, deploying SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and the novel ZOHOMURK implant, which abuses hardcoded Zoho WorkDrive OAuth credentials to use an attacker-controlled cloud account as a command-and-control dead drop that blends with normal traffic. Acronis confirmed active compromises of senior administrative machines, attributes the activity with high confidence based on reused Toneshell code and shared infrastructure, and worked with CERT-In on cleanup.

title: 236,000 DCloud Uni-App Sites Used in Crypto Scams, Phishing, and Wallet Drainers
blurb: Infoblox identified 236,493 distinct second-level domains running investment-scam templates built on DCloud Uni-App, a legitimate Chinese cross-platform framework, powering fake crypto exchanges, pig-butchering operations, WhatsApp phishing, rigged gambling sites, and wallet drainers since mid-2022. Technical fingerprints, victim-communication patterns, and bulletproof hosting suggest centralized ownership across a large share of the sites, including the RainbowEx Ponzi scheme that defrauded tens of thousands in Argentina.

title: Forensic Trajectory Signatures for Agent Memory Poisoning Detection
blurb: Researchers studying LLM agents under persistent memory poisoning found a behavioral invariant: successful exfiltration requires a memory_recall_fact call before email_send_email, a transition benign sessions rarely produce. A Random Forest over 19 trajectory features hits AUC 0.9904 and generalizes to GPT-4.1 and GPT-4o without retraining. For defenders, this means tool-call logs alone can flag memory-channel attacks and distinguish them from prompt injection, which leaves a separate trajectory signature.

title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster Flaw Could Let Attackers Run Root Commands Pre-Auth
blurb: Progress Kemp LoadMaster contains a critical command-injection flaw (CVE-2026-8037, CVSS 9.8) that lets an unauthenticated attacker run arbitrary commands as root by sending a crafted JSON request to the /accessv2 API endpoint. Progress patched GA v7.2.63.2 and LTSF v7.2.54.18 on June 4 with no reported in-the-wild exploitation, but watchTowr published a full exploit chain on June 29, so edge appliances with the API enabled should be updated now.

title: Kali Linux 2026.2 released with 9 new tools, NetHunter updates
blurb: Kali Linux 2026.2 shipped with nine new tools, including legba (a multiprotocol credential bruteforcer and password sprayer), penelope (a shell handler), and tookie-osint for social-media account discovery, plus a re-added hydra-gtk GUI. The release upgrades to kernel 6.19 and strips graphics firmware from pre-built VM images, cutting the initrd to 60 MB and roughly tripling VM boot speed.

title: Nissan says Oracle PeopleSoft break-in may have spilled payroll records, SSNs
blurb: Nissan disclosed that attackers breached its Oracle PeopleSoft environment and may have exfiltrated payroll records and Social Security numbers belonging to current and former employees. The carmaker has not confirmed how the intrusion occurred, but the timing aligns with the wave of Oracle E-Business and PeopleSoft attacks tied to Cl0p, so organizations running internet-facing PeopleSoft should audit access logs and assume data exposure.

title: iOS 26.5.2 has fixes for 25+ security issues on iPhone, details here
blurb: Apple released iOS and iPadOS 26.5.2 and macOS 26.5.2, patching nearly 30 security issues that center on WebKit and WebRTC, with additional kernel-level fixes. The flaws were first addressed in the iOS 26.6 betas; Apple recommends all iPhone, iPad, and Mac users install the update, since web-engine bugs are common drive-by exploitation vectors.

title: New Controller Flaws Expose Highway Signs and Billboards to Remote Hacking
blurb: Three vulnerabilities in Daktronics VFC-DMP-5000, DMP-5000, and DMP-8000 display controllers, including an unauthenticated path traversal and default admin credentials, can give an attacker root-level control over highway signs and digital billboards, per a CISA advisory. Daktronics has shipped patches, but the researcher found most internet-exposed units still using default passwords, so operators should pull controllers off the public internet and rotate credentials immediately.

title: The Bear Necessities: A Look at the Drivers, Dynamics, and Applications of the Pro-Russia Influence Ecosystem
blurb: Google Threat Intelligence Group reports that the pro-Russia influence ecosystem has matured from a wartime tool into a global strategic asset, pivoting from Ukraine-specific goals back toward the EU, NATO, and other long-standing Kremlin targets. The group expects influence operations and hacktivism to intensify outside Ukraine, increasingly powered by generative AI for planning, research, and content creation. Defenders should treat the ecosystem as resilient to narrow takedowns, since its interconnected components survive limited-scope disruption.

title: Nissan Employee Data Breached in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack
blurb: Nissan Americas disclosed a breach from the ShinyHunters zero-day campaign against Oracle PeopleSoft (CVE-2026-35273), exposing data on current and former employees across the US, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil. The stolen records may include SSNs, banking details, and tax and payroll data; PeopleSoft customers should patch CVE-2026-35273, hunt for exploitation, and rotate exposed credentials.

title: India’s central bank mandated use of .bank domains to enhance trust – but its registry leaked sensitive info
blurb: India's Reserve Bank mandated banks migrate to the .bank.in domain to curb phishing and build customer trust, but the registry running the program exposed sensitive applicant data, including documents and contact details submitted during onboarding. Institutions that filed through the registry should treat their submitted records as compromised and watch for targeted phishing that abuses the leaked details to impersonate the very trust scheme meant to prevent it.

title: Blackfield ransomware asks Nidec Corporation for $2 million ransom
blurb: The Blackfield ransomware gang has claimed an attack on Nidec Chaun Choung Technology, a Taiwanese subsidiary of Japanese motor manufacturer Nidec Corporation, and is demanding $2 million to delete allegedly stolen data. Nidec confirmed the June 22 compromise of a subsidiary server and a possible information leak, marking its second ransomware-related breach after 8Base and Everest hit its Vietnam division in 2024.

title: Gamaredon Expands Ukraine Attacks with New Malware and Cloud Service Abuse
blurb: ESET reported that Russian APT Gamaredon ran 35 spear-phishing campaigns against Ukrainian government and military targets in 2025, deploying six new PowerShell tools and weaponizing a patched WinRAR flaw (CVE-2025-8088) to drop HTA downloaders into the Startup folder for persistence. The group increasingly routed command-and-control through tunnel services and serverless worker platforms to mask its back-end infrastructure, so defenders should monitor outbound traffic to these providers and patch WinRAR.

title: Words Speak Louder Than Code: Investigating Cognitive Heuristics in LLM-Based Code Vulnerability Detection
blurb: This paper shows that LLM-based vulnerability detectors are susceptible to the same cognitive heuristics that bias humans, with susceptibility holding code fixed and varying only surrounding context. Across eight models and three languages, framing effects (via task objectives) flipped verdicts 33.2% of the time, anchoring 23.5%, and author-attribution halo 18.4%, with semantic-reasoning bugs more affected than pattern-matchable ones. A black-box cognitive attack suppressed up to 97% of previously detected vulnerabilities, so treat LLM scanner context as an untrusted, manipulable input.

title: In major privacy win, Supreme Court rules geofence warrants are protected by privacy rights
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that geofence warrants fall under Fourth Amendment protection, holding that individuals have a reasonable expectation of privacy in their cell-phone location data and that police must obtain a search warrant to compel companies like Google to hand it over. The court declined to ban geofence warrants outright, instead requiring law enforcement to narrow these "reverse" search requests rather than sweeping up the location records of every nearby user.

title: The Spectrum Strikes Back: Infrared POV Attacks on Traffic Sign Classification
blurb: This paper introduces a persistence-of-vision attack that uses near-infrared light, invisible to humans, to inject dynamic adversarial content onto traffic signs and fool autonomous-vehicle classifiers. Tested against 12 models at distances up to 20 meters under varying illumination, it achieved high success rates; the authors propose near-infrared cutoff filters and software-based detection as defenses.

title: Supreme Court delivers ‘major win’ for tech privacy in Chatrie ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that collecting phone location data from a geographic area constitutes a Fourth Amendment search, extending the Carpenter precedent to geofence warrants. The decision means law enforcement requests for bulk location data from providers like Google now face Fourth Amendment scrutiny, though the court declined to rule on whether Chatrie's specific warrant was valid.

title: iPhone 18 Pro supplier list and parts exposed in Tata data leak
blurb: The ransomware group World Leaks posted files stolen from Tata Electronics, Apple's Indian manufacturing partner, exposing bill-of-materials data for the unreleased iPhone 18 Pro, including component lists, named suppliers, and drop-test photos dated early 2026. At least six files map main-logic-board chips, battery, and camera parts to specific vendors, revealing single-source dependencies and following an earlier Tata breach the company acknowledged but left unverified.

title: Defending Against Harmful Supervision Hidden in Benign Samples
blurb: This paper introduces Embedded Attack, which hides harmful QA pairs inside benign fine-tuning samples and evades example-level guardrails that only catch explicitly mixed-in harmful content. The authors propose Dual-Reference SFT (DR-SFT), a token-level regularization approach adapting DPO-style contrastive objectives to supervised fine-tuning, mitigating the poisoning where coarse data filtering fails.

title: 'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud, AI Credentials
blurb: Djinn Stealer is being deployed via CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication-bypass in the SimpleHelp RMM platform, which an attacker exploited to obtain an authenticated technician session and mass-deploy an obfuscated JavaScript loader (TaskWeaver) across endpoints. The payload sweeps developer machines for cloud credentials, SSH keys, API keys, and package-registry tokens (npm, PyPI, Maven, NuGet), so organizations running internet-facing SimpleHelp should patch immediately and rotate any exposed infrastructure and registry secrets.

title: Your Space is My Zone: Demystifying the Security Risks of AI-Powered Applications on Pre-Trained Model Hubs
blurb: Researchers published the first systematic security analysis of AI-powered apps on Hugging Face and two other model hubs, mapping five threat categories and ten attack vectors including broken access control, insecure resource reuse, and input injection enabling arbitrary code execution. Their Insightor framework scanned over 970,000 public apps and found thousands leaking credentials, hundreds with code-execution flaws, and tens carrying embedded backdoors that indicate active exploitation.

title: The Supreme Court just made it harder for police to track your phone
blurb: The US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that geofence searches of phone-location data constitute a Fourth Amendment search, requiring a warrant backed by probable cause. The decision extends the 2018 Carpenter precedent and does not ban geofence warrants outright, but it forces police to name a specific basis and narrow requests rather than sweeping every device in an area.

title: Inside the Advisory Database and what happens when vulnerability volume breaks records
blurb: GitHub's Advisory Database published 1,560 reviewed advisories in May 2026, more than 5x its typical monthly output and the highest on record. Private vulnerability reports jumped from ~550/week in January to over 3,000/week, and GitHub CNA CVE requests hit nearly 4,000 in May, roughly 10x year-over-year. Review times are now longer, but advisories remain human-validated. Defenders can help by submitting complete vulnerability data, coordinating with maintainers, and requesting CVEs only with clear intent to publish.

title: US offers $10m bounty for info on Russia-linked hackers behind Signal and WhatsApp attacks
blurb: The US State Department offered up to $10M for information identifying members of UNC5792 (FSB-linked) and UNC4221 (Russian military intelligence), which phished the Signal and WhatsApp accounts of US and allied government, defense, and diplomatic personnel. The groups abused legitimate device-linking features via social engineering rather than encryption flaws, compromising thousands of accounts; CISA and the FBI warn the actors have shifted toward harvesting victims' backup recovery keys.

title: A Lightweight Post-Quantum Authentication Framework for 5G Base Station Bootstrapping
blurb: This paper introduces EMULSION, a symmetric-key framework for authenticating 5G base station SIB broadcasts, which 5G leaves unprotected and open to fake base station attacks, MITM interception, and spoofed emergency alerts. It uses a TESLA-style HMAC chain anchored by a per-epoch MAYO post-quantum signature, fitting authentication into a single SIB packet with no fragmentation while covering SIB1 through SIB21. On an over-the-air testbed it ran 33x faster than ML-DSA and 12x faster than FN-DSA, both of which exceed 5G packet-size limits.

title: On the Internet, Nobody Knows You're an LLM Bot: Unmasking Web Agents with Multi-Layer Fingerprinting
blurb: Researchers deployed honeysites guarded by CAPTCHAs, proof-of-work, and Cloudflare's anti-bot defenses, then pointed six LLM-based web agents at them, finding that some bypassed every mechanism tested. Multi-layer fingerprinting across the network, HTTP, and browser layers reliably separated the agents from humans and from each other. The counterintuitive takeaway for defenders: stealth and anti-detection add-ons usually raise an agent's detectability rather than lower it, so layered fingerprinting beats blocklists for spotting LLM-driven traffic.

title: Security researchers tricked LLMs into giving them cocaine recipes by abusing role models for prompt injection
blurb: Security researchers bypassed LLM safety guardrails to extract prohibited content, including drug-synthesis instructions, by abusing role-play and persona prompts to smuggle in prompt injections. The work underscores that current alignment filters remain reactive and easily circumvented, so teams deploying LLMs in production should layer input/output content filtering and treat model-side guardrails as insufficient on their own.

title: Proofs of Ownership for Machine Learning Models
blurb: This paper formalizes proofs of ownership for stolen ML models as a three-party game among an owner, a thief, and a judge, where the owner perturbs a model and attaches a proof, and the judge decides whether a suspect model derives from it. The authors prove a dichotomy for black-box classifiers: under standard cryptographic assumptions, ownership is provable if and only if the concept class is not self-correctable. The result gives a precise boundary for when model-theft attribution is achievable.

title: What the Saga Over Anthropic’s Mythos Tells Us About the Cyber Risks From AI
blurb: Anthropic has restricted its Mythos model to 200 partner organizations, saying its ability to find software vulnerabilities is too dangerous for general release. The company warned that broad access could let attackers more easily steal data or disrupt critical infrastructure, framing the limited rollout as a guardrail against offensive misuse.

title: Microsoft extends Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027
blurb: Microsoft extended hotpatching support for Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition through October 2027, one year past the original October 2026 end date. The extension applies only to systems enrolled in hotpatch updates, which receive monthly security patches by modifying in-memory process code without requiring a restart, reducing reboot-driven downtime during vulnerability response.

title: Microsoft to assist European Commission in defense of EU-US data-sharing agreement
blurb: Microsoft agreed to support the European Commission's legal defense of the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, the transatlantic agreement that lets companies move Europeans' personal data to US servers under approved safeguards. A court strike-down would invalidate the legal basis many cloud and SaaS providers rely on for EU-to-US transfers, forcing security and compliance teams back to standard contractual clauses and fresh transfer-risk assessments.
